question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has developed gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea. She is following her sick-day plan but is unsure when to contact her healthcare provider immediately. According to standard diabetes sick-day management guidelines, which of the following situations would warrant immediate contact with her healthcare team?

options.A: If her blood glucose levels are consistently above 180 mg/dL for more than 6 hours, regardless of ketone status or ability to keep fluids down.
options.B: If she needs to temporarily reduce her basal insulin dose by 50% to prevent hypoglycemia while she is eating less than usual.
options.C: If she experiences mild to moderate ketones (trace to small on urine strips) but her blood glucose is below 200 mg/dL, as this indicates she needs more carbohydrates.

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years has well-controlled blood glucose (HbA1c 6.8%) and no visual symptoms. She has never had a diabetic retinopathy screening. According to current guidelines, when should she have her first dilated comprehensive eye examination?

options.B: She can wait until she develops visual symptoms, since her diabetes is well-controlled and she is asymptomatic.
options.C: At age 50, as routine screening should begin then regardless of diabetes duration.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 45-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 31 kg/m², no cardiovascular disease) presents for initial treatment planning. He has no contraindications to metformin. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start insulin therapy immediately to achieve rapid glycemic control and preserve beta-cell function.

options.C: Initiate a GLP-1 receptor agonist as first-line therapy because of its weight loss benefits, regardless of cardiovascular risk status.

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with gastroenteritis. She asks why her diabetes educator emphasized drinking plenty of fluids while sick. Which of the following best explains the rationale for this recommendation?

options.A: Drinking large amounts of water helps flush excess glucose from the bloodstream through increased urine output, which is the primary mechanism for lowering blood sugar during illness.
options.B: Extra fluids are needed to prevent diabetic ketoacidosis by diluting ketones in the blood, as dehydration alone causes ketone production regardless of blood glucose levels.
options.C: When sick, people with diabetes should drink only sugar-free fluids to avoid hyperglycemia, since eating is usually reduced during illness.

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 180-220 mg/dL) despite appropriate basal rate adjustments. options.A: Immediately increase all basal rates by 20% and bolus insulin doses by 15% to overcome insulin resistance.
options.B: Discontinue the pump and switch back to multiple daily injections for at least one week to 'reset' insulin sensitivity.
options.C: Assume the hyperglycemia is due to dawn phenomenon and program a temporary basal rate increase only from 3 AM to 7 AM.

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with gastroenteritis and poor oral intake. She asks about appropriate gelatin consumption during this illness. Which of the following recommendations is correct?

options.A: She should consume only sugar-free gelatin, as regular gelatin contains too much sugar and will dangerously raise her blood glucose during illness.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin products while sick and instead only consume clear broth, as gelatin provides no meaningful nutritional benefit for diabetes management during illness.
options.C: She should consume gelatin mixed with extra protein powder to prevent hypoglycemia, since illness increases protein requirements and gelatin alone lacks sufficient protein.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent fasting hyperglycemia (180-220 mg/dL) despite appropriate basal rate settings. options.B: Advise her to disconnect the pump overnight and take a single injection of long-acting insulin instead, as pumps are unreliable for overnight control.
options.C: Tell her to administer correction boluses every 2 hours throughout the night until fasting glucose normalizes, without investigating the cause.

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the clinic for follow-up. She reports experiencing frequent episodes of hypoglycemia (blood glucose <70 mg/dL) in the late afternoon. She is currently on a basal-bolus insulin regimen. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial management strategy to address this pattern?

options.B: Instruct her to stop her basal insulin entirely for one day to 'reset' her system, then restart at a 15% lower dose the following day.
options.C: Recommend she switch her rapid-acting insulin to a regular insulin formulation before meals, as its longer duration of action will provide more stable coverage and prevent late-afternoon drops.

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes asks about calorie-free, caffeine-free liquids she can consume throughout the day to stay hydrated without affecting her blood glucose. Which of the following recommendations is appropriate?

options.C: Clear sports drinks without added sugar are ideal as they provide electrolytes without calories, helping prevent dehydration-related glucose fluctuations.

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years, well-controlled on metformin with an HbA1c of 6.8%, presents for her annual diabetes review. She has no visual symptoms. According to current guidelines, what is the appropriate recommendation for diabetic retinopathy screening?

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes develops gastroenteritis with vomiting. She asks why her diabetes management plan emphasizes increased fluid intake during illness. Which of the following best explains the rationale?

question: A 52-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) and no cardiovascular disease presents for initial management. options.B: Begin insulin therapy immediately to achieve rapid glycemic control and preserve beta-cell function
options.C: Prescribe a sulfonylurea as first-line therapy due to its potent glucose-lowering effects and low cost

question: A 45-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years, well-controlled on metformin with an HbA1c of 6.8%, presents for routine care. question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and a long-acting insulin is experiencing nausea and vomiting due to gastroenteritis. question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (blood glucose consistently >250 mg/dL) despite multiple correction boluses over the past 6 hours. question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes asks why her endocrinologist strongly recommends she wear a medical identification bracelet. Which of the following best explains the primary clinical rationale for this recommendation?

question: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 31 kg/m², no cardiovascular disease) presents for initial treatment planning. question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the emergency department with hypoglycemia (glucose 48 mg/dL). She is conscious but confused. After confirming her airway, breathing, and circulation, what is the most appropriate immediate management?

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (blood glucose consistently >250 mg/dL) for the past 24 hours. question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with gastroenteritis. When counseling her about fluid management during illness, which of the following statements is correct regarding the importance of adequate hydration?

options.A: Drinking large amounts of plain water is essential to flush out excess ketones and lower blood glucose levels more effectively than other fluids.
options.B: Fluid intake should be restricted if vomiting occurs to prevent worsening of nausea and to avoid fluid overload, especially in patients with diabetes.
options.C: The primary goal is to prevent hypoglycemia by maintaining blood volume, so sugary drinks should be consumed exclusively regardless of current blood glucose levels.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. She reports excellent glycemic control with a time-in-range of 85% and an A1c of 6.8%. During the visit, she mentions she's planning a 3-day hiking trip in a remote area. What is the most appropriate recommendation regarding her insulin pump management during this activity?

options.A: Advise her to disconnect the pump entirely during the hike and take subcutaneous insulin injections instead, as pumps are too fragile for outdoor activities.
options.B: Recommend she increase her basal rate by 50% during the hike to prevent hyperglycemia from the physical exertion.
options.C: Suggest she set a temporary basal rate reduction of 80% starting 2 hours before the hike to prevent hypoglycemia during exercise.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the clinic for routine follow-up. She reports experiencing occasional hypoglycemic episodes at home, typically in the late afternoon. When asked about her management of these episodes, which of the following approaches would be considered appropriate?

options.A: Consume a high-protein snack like cheese or nuts immediately when symptoms begin, as protein provides sustained energy without rapid glucose spikes.
options.B: Take 1-2 units of rapid-acting insulin when feeling hypoglycemic to help stabilize blood glucose levels through enhanced cellular uptake.
options.C: Lie down and rest for 30 minutes without eating anything, as most mild hypoglycemic episodes resolve spontaneously with rest.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is experiencing nausea and vomiting due to a viral gastroenteritis. She is able to keep down small amounts of clear liquids and asks about using gelatin to help with hydration and energy. Which of the following recommendations about gelatin selection during illness is most appropriate?

options.A: She should choose sugar-free gelatin exclusively, as regular gelatin contains too much carbohydrate and will dangerously raise her blood glucose during illness.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin products and instead only consume plain water, as any carbohydrate intake during illness will increase the risk of diabetic ketoacidosis.
options.C: She should choose gelatin with the highest sugar content available to provide maximal energy, since illness increases metabolic demands and hypoglycemia risk is the primary concern.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 31 kg/m²) presents for initial treatment planning. She has no cardiovascular disease, normal renal function, and expresses strong motivation for lifestyle changes. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start immediate insulin therapy to achieve rapid glycemic control and 'rest the pancreas' for better long-term function.
options.B: Prescribe metformin only if lifestyle modifications fail to lower HbA1c below 7.0% after 6 months, as medication should be a last resort.
options.C: Begin a sulfonylurea as first-line therapy due to its potent glucose-lowering effects, then add metformin only if needed for additional control.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 180-220 mg/dL) for 3 days. She reports no symptoms of illness, has not changed her diet, and her pump is delivering insulin normally according to self-checks. What is the most appropriate initial step in troubleshooting?

options.A: Immediately increase all basal rates by 20% and bolus insulin doses by 15% to correct the pattern, then reassess in 24 hours.
options.B: Discontinue pump use and switch to multiple daily injections with long-acting insulin at 80% of the total daily pump dose, as pumps often fail during hyperglycemic episodes.
options.C: Assume the hyperglycemia is due to insulin resistance from an undetected infection and start a course of broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes is concerned about dehydration during a heat wave. She asks her primary care provider which calorie-free, caffeine-free beverages she can safely consume in unlimited quantities to stay hydrated. Which of the following recommendations would be appropriate?

options.A: Diet sodas containing artificial sweeteners like aspartame are completely safe and can be consumed freely as they have no effect on blood glucose or insulin secretion.
options.B: Fruit-infused water with lemon or cucumber slices is recommended, but she should limit it to 2 glasses daily since the natural fruit acids may stimulate insulin release even without calories.
options.C: Clear sports drinks labeled 'zero sugar' are ideal for hydration as they replace electrolytes lost through sweating without affecting blood glucose levels.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has developed gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea for 12 hours. According to standard sick-day management guidelines, which of the following situations would require her to contact her healthcare provider immediately?

options.A: If her blood glucose levels are consistently below 180 mg/dL, as this indicates her insulin doses should be reduced to prevent hypoglycemia during illness.
options.B: If she is unable to keep down any food or fluids for more than 4 hours, but her blood glucose remains stable with her usual insulin regimen.
options.C: If she develops moderate ketones in her urine but feels well enough to continue her normal activities and monitor at home.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with gastroenteritis and vomiting for 24 hours. She asks why her diabetes management plan emphasizes aggressive fluid intake during this illness. Which of the following best explains the rationale?

options.A: Drinking large volumes of water will directly lower blood glucose by diluting the bloodstream, preventing hyperglycemia.
options.B: Extra fluids are needed primarily to flush out excess ketones through the kidneys, which is the main mechanism for preventing diabetic ketoacidosis.
options.C: Increased fluid intake prevents hypoglycemia by maintaining adequate blood volume for insulin absorption and action.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years presents for routine follow-up. Her HbA1c is 7.2%, and she has no visual symptoms. She has never had a diabetic retinopathy screening. According to current guidelines, when should she have her first diabetic retinopathy screening?

options.A: She should have screening now, but only if she reports any visual symptoms like blurriness or floaters.
options.B: She should have screening immediately, and if it's normal, she can wait 5 years before the next one.
options.C: Since she is under 50 and has good glycemic control, screening can be deferred until she turns 50.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and a long-acting insulin is experiencing vomiting and diarrhea due to acute gastroenteritis. She asks what type of gelatin she should consume to help stay hydrated. Based on best practices for sick-day management in diabetes, what guidance should be provided?

options.A: She should choose sugar-free gelatin exclusively, as any sugar intake during illness will dangerously raise her blood glucose and should be avoided completely.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin and instead only drink plain water, since gelatin contains carbohydrates that could interfere with her insulin regimen during illness.
options.C: She should use regular gelatin with added sugar, but must simultaneously double her usual long-acting insulin dose to cover the expected carbohydrate load from the gelatin.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) and no cardiovascular disease presents for initial management. He has no contraindications to metformin. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start insulin therapy immediately to achieve rapid glycemic control and preserve beta-cell function.
options.B: Begin with a sulfonylurea like glipizide as first-line therapy due to its potent glucose-lowering effects.
options.C: Initiate a GLP-1 receptor agonist as first-line therapy given its cardiovascular benefits, even without established cardiovascular disease.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. She reports occasional unexplained hyperglycemia despite consistent carbohydrate counting. Her pump data shows several episodes of hyperglycemia occurring 2-3 hours after meals. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial adjustment to address this pattern?

options.A: Increase the basal insulin rate by 20% across all time periods to provide better background coverage
options.B: Switch to a fixed carbohydrate ratio for all meals rather than adjusting for different meal types
options.C: Program a temporary basal rate increase for 4 hours after each meal to cover postprandial spikes
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has developed gastroenteritis with vomiting. Her diabetes educator emphasizes the importance of maintaining adequate fluid intake during this illness. Which of the following best explains the primary rationale for this recommendation?

options.A: Drinking large amounts of water will directly lower blood glucose levels by diluting the glucose in the bloodstream, preventing hyperglycemia.
options.B: Extra fluids are needed primarily to flush out ketones through the kidneys, so sugary drinks should be avoided completely to prevent worsening hyperglycemia.
options.C: Increased hydration helps prevent hypoglycemia by ensuring adequate blood volume for insulin absorption and action during illness.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 180-220 mg/dL) despite appropriate basal rate settings. She reports no recent illness, stress, or dietary changes. Her pump site is on her abdomen, changed 2 days ago. What is the most appropriate next step in troubleshooting?

options.A: Immediately increase all basal rates by 20% and program a temporary basal rate of 150% for the next 6 hours to correct the hyperglycemia.
options.B: Discontinue the pump and switch back to multiple daily injections with long-acting insulin for at least one week to 'reset' insulin sensitivity before returning to pump therapy.
options.C: Continue current settings but add a scheduled correction bolus every 4 hours regardless of glucose readings until values return to target range.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is discussing emergency preparedness with her primary care provider. Which of the following represents the most important reason for consistently wearing a medical identification tag or bracelet?

options.A: It allows paramedics to immediately administer a standard 50% dextrose IV bolus for any altered mental status, preventing the need for fingerstick glucose testing in the field.
options.B: It serves as legal documentation that exempts the wearer from liability if they experience a hypoglycemic episode while driving or operating machinery.
options.C: It contains encrypted medical records that emergency departments can scan to access the patient's complete insulin regimen and recent HbA1c values, replacing the need for verbal history.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. She reports frequent hypoglycemia, especially overnight. Her pump download shows basal rates of 1.2 units/hour from midnight to 6 AM, with carbohydrate ratios of 1:8 for breakfast and 1:10 for lunch/dinner. Her time-in-range is 65%, with 12% below range. Which adjustment would be most appropriate to address her overnight hypoglycemia?

options.A: Increase the overnight basal rate by 0.2 units/hour to prevent glucose spikes that lead to reactive hypoglycemia
options.B: Switch to a fixed carbohydrate ratio of 1:10 for all meals to simplify management and reduce insulin stacking
options.C: Discontinue the pump temporarily and return to multiple daily injections until hypoglycemia resolves
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 31 kg/m², no cardiovascular disease) presents for initial treatment planning. She expresses a strong preference to avoid injections. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start metformin immediately, but also initiate basal insulin at bedtime to achieve rapid glycemic control and 'rest the pancreas'.
options.B: Prescribe a sulfonylurea (e.g., glipizide) as first-line monotherapy, as it provides the strongest HbA1c reduction and addresses her aversion to injections.
options.C: Begin a GLP-1 receptor agonist (e.g., semaglutide) as first-line monotherapy, given her BMI and the drug's cardiovascular benefits, despite her preference for oral agents.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has developed gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea for the past 12 hours. She is following her sick-day protocol but is unsure when to contact her healthcare provider urgently. According to standard diabetes sick-day management guidelines, which of the following situations would warrant immediate medical contact?

options.A: If her blood glucose levels are consistently below 180 mg/dL, she should call immediately because this indicates her insulin doses are too high during illness.
options.B: She should wait to contact her provider until she has had persistent hyperglycemia (blood glucose >250 mg/dL) for at least 48 hours, as shorter durations can be managed with increased fluids alone.
options.C: Immediate contact is only necessary if she develops a fever above 101°F; otherwise, she can continue monitoring at home regardless of other symptoms.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the emergency department with hypoglycemia (glucose 48 mg/dL). She is conscious but confused. After confirming hypoglycemia, what is the most appropriate initial management?

options.A: Administer 1 mg of intramuscular glucagon immediately, then wait 15 minutes to reassess glucose before giving oral carbohydrates.
options.B: Give 4 ounces of orange juice orally, then administer her usual dose of rapid-acting insulin to prevent rebound hyperglycemia.
options.C: Start a continuous intravenous dextrose infusion at 100 mL/hour of D10W, as this provides a steady glucose supply without risk of overcorrection.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes develops gastroenteritis with vomiting. Her blood glucose readings have been elevated at 250-300 mg/dL for the past 12 hours. When counseling her about fluid intake during this illness, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: She should drink only plain water to avoid adding extra calories that might raise her blood sugar further, even if this means drinking less fluid overall.
options.B: She should limit fluid intake to prevent dilutional hyponatremia since her blood glucose is already high, and focus instead on adjusting her insulin doses.
options.C: She should drink sugary beverages like regular soda or juice to provide energy since she's vomiting and not eating, and this will help prevent hypoglycemia.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 31 kg/m², no comorbidities) presents for initial management. He is asymptomatic and has normal renal function. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start insulin glargine immediately to achieve rapid glycemic control and preserve beta-cell function.
options.B: Begin with a sulfonylurea such as glipizide as first-line therapy since it's inexpensive and effective at lowering glucose.
options.C: Prescribe metformin only if lifestyle modifications fail after 6 months of documented non-response.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and a GLP-1 receptor agonist develops gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea. She asks what type of gelatin she should consume to help stay hydrated. Based on current diabetes sick-day management guidelines, what is the appropriate recommendation?

options.A: She should choose sugar-free gelatin exclusively, as any sugar intake during illness will dangerously elevate her blood glucose and should be avoided.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin products and instead only consume clear broth, as gelatin contains carbohydrates that are inappropriate during diabetic illness.
options.C: She should use regular gelatin sweetened with sugar, but must immediately take an extra dose of her GLP-1 receptor agonist to counteract the expected blood sugar rise.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 180-220 mg/dL) despite increasing her basal rates. She reports no symptoms of illness, and her carbohydrate counting appears accurate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in troubleshooting?

options.A: Immediately increase all basal rates by 20% and advise her to continue this adjustment for at least one week before reassessment.
options.B: Recommend she discontinue the pump and return to multiple daily injections with long-acting insulin, as the pump is clearly malfunctioning.
options.C: Instruct her to check for ketones only if her glucose exceeds 250 mg/dL, and focus troubleshooting solely on the pump's insulin delivery settings.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. Her time-in-range over the past 14 days is 65%, with frequent postprandial hyperglycemia. She reports consistent carbohydrate counting and uses the pump's bolus calculator for meals. Which of the following would be an appropriate initial adjustment to improve her postprandial glucose control?

options.A: Increase her basal insulin rate by 20% across all time segments to provide more background insulin coverage for meals.
options.B: Advise her to take all meal boluses 60 minutes before eating to allow insulin absorption to peak right at meal start.
options.C: Switch her to a fixed meal bolus amount for all meals regardless of carbohydrate content, as the pump's calculations appear inaccurate.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has developed gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea for 12 hours. She is following her sick-day rules but is unsure when to contact her healthcare provider immediately. Based on standard diabetes sick-day management guidelines, which of the following situations would warrant immediate contact?

options.A: If her blood glucose levels are consistently below 180 mg/dL, as this indicates she's not eating enough and needs insulin adjustment to prevent hypoglycemia.
options.B: If she's able to keep down small sips of water but has skipped two insulin doses to avoid hypoglycemia while not eating normally.
options.C: If she develops moderate ketones in her urine but her blood glucose is only 150 mg/dL, so she should wait to see if it resolves on its own.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years, well-controlled on metformin (HbA1c 6.8%), presents for her annual checkup. She has no visual symptoms. According to current guidelines, what is the appropriate recommendation for diabetic retinopathy screening?

options.A: No screening is needed since she has no visual symptoms and her diabetes is well-controlled.
options.B: She should have a comprehensive dilated eye exam every 2 years since her HbA1c is below 7%.
options.C: She should be screened only when she develops hypertension or other microvascular complications.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with a viral gastroenteritis illness. She is experiencing nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. When counseling her about fluid management during this illness, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: She should drink only clear fluids like water and avoid electrolyte-containing beverages to prevent sodium overload, as people with diabetes are prone to hypertension.
options.B: She should limit fluid intake to small sips of water to avoid diluting her blood and causing falsely low glucose readings on her continuous glucose monitor.
options.C: She should drink sugary fluids like regular soda or juice exclusively to maintain her blood glucose levels, since she's not eating normally.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. Her time-in-range (TIR) over the past 14 days is 65%, with frequent postprandial hyperglycemia. Her current total daily insulin dose is 40 units, with a carbohydrate ratio of 1:10 and correction factor of 1:50. Which of the following adjustments would be most appropriate to improve her postprandial glucose control?

options.A: Increase the basal insulin rate by 20% across all time segments to provide more background insulin coverage for meals
options.B: Switch to a fixed pre-meal bolus of 5 units for all meals regardless of carbohydrate content to simplify management
options.C: Discontinue the insulin pump and return to multiple daily injections with NPH insulin twice daily to provide better peak coverage
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes asks why her endocrinologist strongly recommends she wear a medical ID bracelet. Which of the following statements provides the most accurate rationale for this recommendation?

options.A: It allows paramedics to immediately administer a standard 15g glucose gel to all unconscious patients with diabetes, preventing the need for blood sugar testing.
options.B: It serves as a legal document that absolves healthcare providers from liability if they administer insulin during a hypoglycemic emergency.
options.C: It primarily functions to alert restaurant staff about dietary restrictions so they can automatically provide sugar-free options when dining out.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (blood glucose consistently >250 mg/dL) for the past 12 hours. She reports no recent illness, has been eating her usual diet, and has changed her infusion set this morning. What is the most appropriate initial action?

options.A: Immediately increase her basal insulin rate by 50% for the next 24 hours to correct the hyperglycemia, then reassess.
options.B: Administer a subcutaneous injection of long-acting insulin while continuing the pump therapy to ensure adequate background insulin coverage.
options.C: Discontinue the pump and switch to multiple daily injections using her current insulin-to-carbohydrate ratios and correction factors.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes is concerned about hydration and asks her primary care provider which beverages she can consume freely without affecting her blood glucose. Which of the following recommendations would be appropriate?

options.A: Diet sodas are completely safe as they contain no sugar, and the artificial sweeteners have no metabolic effects.
options.B: Fruit juices diluted with water are acceptable since the dilution reduces the sugar concentration enough to avoid blood sugar spikes.
options.C: Caffeinated, unsweetened tea and coffee are not recommended because caffeine always causes dehydration in people with diabetes.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years, well-controlled on metformin (HbA1c 6.8%), presents for her annual physical. She has no visual symptoms. According to current guidelines, when should she have her next diabetic retinopathy screening?

options.A: She should have annual dilated fundus exams by an ophthalmologist starting at diagnosis of type 2 diabetes, regardless of glycemic control.
options.B: Since her HbA1c is below 7.0%, she can defer screening for 3 years and only needs immediate screening if she develops visual symptoms.
options.C: She should have screening only if her primary care provider detects any abnormality on direct ophthalmoscopy during routine physical exams.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with hypoglycemia (blood glucose 52 mg/dL) and mild neuroglycopenic symptoms. She is alert and able to swallow safely. According to current guidelines, what is the appropriate initial management?

options.A: Administer 1 mg of intramuscular glucagon immediately, as this provides the fastest and most reliable glucose elevation in conscious patients.
options.B: Give 4 ounces of orange juice followed by a complex carbohydrate like whole-wheat crackers to prevent rebound hypoglycemia.
options.C: Have her consume 15 grams of pure glucose tablets, then recheck blood glucose in 30 minutes; if still low, repeat with another 15 grams.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with gastroenteritis and poor oral intake. She asks about appropriate gelatin choices during her illness. Which of the following recommendations is correct?

options.A: She should choose sugar-free gelatin exclusively, as it contains no carbohydrates and will not affect her blood glucose levels during illness.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin products while sick and instead consume only clear broth, as gelatin contains hidden sugars that will inevitably cause hyperglycemia.
options.C: She should select regular gelatin with added sugar, as the carbohydrates will provide necessary energy and prevent hypoglycemia, and she can simply increase her insulin dose proportionally to cover it.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the emergency department with hypoglycemia (blood glucose 48 mg/dL). She is conscious but confused and unable to swallow safely. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial management?

options.A: Administer 15 grams of oral glucose gel rubbed on her gums, as this bypasses the swallowing reflex and is absorbed through the oral mucosa.
options.B: Give her a glass of orange juice to drink slowly, as liquid carbohydrates are absorbed faster than solid foods and pose minimal aspiration risk.
options.C: Administer 1 mg of intramuscular glucagon immediately, then reassess her glucose level in 15 minutes before considering additional treatment.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes asks about calorie-free, caffeine-free beverages she can consume without affecting her blood glucose. Which of the following recommendations is appropriate?

options.A: Diet sodas are completely safe as they contain artificial sweeteners that have no metabolic effects and can be consumed in unlimited quantities.
options.B: Fruit-infused water is an excellent choice because the natural sugars from fruits don't count as calories when infused, and it provides beneficial antioxidants without affecting blood sugar.
options.C: Clear sports drinks without added sugar are ideal as they provide electrolytes without calories and help prevent dehydration-related glucose fluctuations.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years presents for routine follow-up. Her HbA1c has been 7.2-7.8% over the past year. She has no visual symptoms. According to current guidelines, when should she next be screened for diabetic retinopathy?

options.A: She should be screened immediately with fluorescein angiography, as her HbA1c is above 7.0% and she is at high risk for vision-threatening retinopathy.
options.B: Since she has no visual symptoms, screening can be deferred until her HbA1c is consistently below 7.0% for at least 6 months.
options.C: She should undergo annual screening with a comprehensive dilated eye exam by an ophthalmologist, regardless of her glycemic control.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and a GLP-1 receptor agonist is experiencing nausea and vomiting due to a viral gastroenteritis. She asks what type of gelatin she should consume to help with hydration and calorie intake while sick. Which of the following recommendations is appropriate?

options.A: She should choose sugar-free gelatin exclusively, as any sugar intake during illness will cause dangerous hyperglycemia and should be avoided completely.
options.B: She should select regular gelatin with sugar, as the sugar content is necessary to prevent hypoglycemia during illness, and she can simply increase her insulin dose to compensate.
options.C: She should avoid all gelatin products and instead only consume clear broth, as gelatin contains carbohydrates that are too difficult to manage during acute illness.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is discussing emergency preparedness with her primary care physician. Which of the following statements accurately describes the primary rationale for consistently wearing medical identification in this context?

options.A: It allows emergency responders to administer a standard 50% dextrose IV push immediately upon arrival, as this is the universal first-line treatment for all diabetic emergencies.
options.B: It serves as a legal document that authorizes bystanders to administer the patient's personal insulin in case of hyperglycemia, preventing the need for emergency medical services.
options.C: It primarily functions to alert healthcare providers to check for peripheral neuropathy and retinopathy first when the patient is unconscious, as these are the most urgent diabetes-related complications in emergency settings.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 180-220 mg/dL) despite appropriate basal rate adjustments. She reports her pump is functioning normally with no occlusion alarms. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in troubleshooting?

options.A: Immediately increase all basal rates by 20% and advise her to continue using the current infusion set for another 2 days to confirm the pattern
options.B: Recommend she disconnects her pump overnight to 'reset' insulin sensitivity and rely on long-acting insulin injections temporarily
options.C: Advise her to stop checking glucose levels for 48 hours to reduce stress-related hyperglycemia, then resume monitoring
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years has well-controlled blood glucose (HbA1c 6.8%) and no visual symptoms. She has never had a diabetic retinopathy screening. According to current guidelines, what is the appropriate recommendation for her initial diabetic retinopathy screening?

options.A: She should have a comprehensive dilated eye exam by an ophthalmologist annually starting immediately, regardless of her glycemic control.
options.B: Since she has no visual symptoms and good glycemic control, screening can be deferred until she develops symptoms or her HbA1c rises above 7.5%.
options.C: She should have a screening fundus photograph through her primary care provider's office, and if normal, she won't need another screening for 3 years.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with unexplained hyperglycemia (blood glucose consistently >250 mg/dL) for the past 24 hours. She reports no recent illness, dietary changes, or stress. She has checked ketones (small) and changed her infusion set yesterday. What is the most appropriate initial action?

options.A: Immediately increase all basal rates by 30% for the next 24 hours to correct the hyperglycemia, then reassess.
options.B: Administer a subcutaneous injection of long-acting insulin as a 'backup' while continuing the pump, then check glucose in 6 hours.
options.C: Discontinue the pump and switch to multiple daily injections using her usual total daily dose divided into basal and bolus insulin.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (blood glucose consistently >250 mg/dL) for the past 24 hours. She reports no recent illness, has been eating her usual diet, and has not changed her insulin settings. On physical exam, she appears well-hydrated and has no signs of infection. What is the most appropriate initial step in troubleshooting her insulin pump?

options.A: Immediately increase her basal insulin rate by 20% across all time segments to bring glucose levels down quickly, then reassess in 6 hours.
options.B: Disconnect the pump and administer a subcutaneous injection of long-acting insulin to provide background coverage while troubleshooting.
options.C: Assume the insulin has degraded due to heat exposure and replace the insulin in the reservoir with a fresh vial, but continue using the same infusion set.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) and no cardiovascular disease presents for initial management. He has a BMI of 31 kg/m² and normal renal function. Which of the following would be appropriate initial pharmacologic treatment?

options.A: Start insulin glargine immediately to achieve rapid glycemic control and preserve beta-cell function.
options.B: Prescribe a sulfonylurea as first-line therapy since it's the most cost-effective option and provides immediate glucose lowering.
options.C: Begin a DPP-4 inhibitor alone, as it has the lowest risk of hypoglycemia and doesn't require monitoring.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has gastroenteritis with vomiting. Her blood glucose is 210 mg/dL. When counseling her about fluid intake during illness, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: She should drink only plain water to avoid raising her blood sugar further, even if this means drinking less overall.
options.B: She should restrict all fluids to prevent worsening vomiting and focus on taking extra insulin instead to control her blood glucose.
options.C: She should drink sugary sodas exclusively to provide both hydration and energy since she's not eating solid foods.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for follow-up. She reports frequent nocturnal hypoglycemia (2-3 AM) over the past month. Her current basal rate is set at 1.0 units/hour from midnight to 6 AM. Her continuous glucose monitor (CGM) data shows consistent glucose levels dropping from 120 mg/dL at midnight to 55 mg/dL by 2 AM. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial adjustment to her insulin pump settings?

options.A: Increase the midnight to 6 AM basal rate to 1.2 units/hour to prevent hyperglycemia before the drop occurs
options.B: Set a temporary basal rate of 0% for 2 hours starting at 1 AM to completely stop insulin delivery during the hypoglycemic period
options.C: Program a higher carbohydrate ratio for her bedtime snack and increase her evening meal bolus to create a glucose buffer
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with symptoms of hypoglycemia (sweating, palpitations, confusion) and a measured blood glucose of 52 mg/dL (2.9 mmol/L). She is conscious but confused. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial management?

options.A: Administer 15-20 grams of complex carbohydrates like whole grain bread or crackers to provide sustained glucose release.
options.B: Give 1 mg of glucagon intramuscularly immediately, as this is the fastest way to raise blood glucose in conscious patients.
options.C: Have her drink 4 ounces of orange juice and wait 30 minutes before rechecking her blood glucose.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old South Asian man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 29 kg/m²) presents for initial management. He has no cardiovascular disease, normal renal function, and is not on other medications. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start immediate insulin therapy to achieve rapid glycemic control and 'rest the pancreas'
options.B: Begin with a sulfonylurea as first-line therapy due to its proven long-term cardiovascular benefits
options.C: Prescribe metformin only if lifestyle modifications fail after 6 months of documented non-adherence
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes asks for guidance on calorie-free, caffeine-free beverages she can consume freely throughout the day to stay hydrated without affecting her blood glucose. Which of the following recommendations would be appropriate?

options.A: Diet sodas with artificial sweeteners are completely safe and calorie-free, and their phosphoric acid content helps prevent kidney stones, which is beneficial for diabetes.
options.B: Fruit-infused water with lemon or cucumber slices is calorie-free and provides essential vitamins without affecting blood sugar, making it superior to plain water.
options.C: Clear broth and bone broth are excellent choices as they are calorie-free, provide electrolytes, and the collagen in bone broth improves insulin sensitivity.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (blood glucose consistently >250 mg/dL) for the past 24 hours. She reports no recent illness, has been eating her usual diet, and has not changed her insulin settings. She has checked her pump and reports it is functioning normally with insulin flowing through the tubing. What is the most appropriate next step in troubleshooting?

options.A: Immediately increase the basal insulin rate by 20% for the next 24 hours to overcome insulin resistance, then reassess.
options.B: Since the pump appears functional, advise her to take a correction bolus every hour until glucose normalizes, without changing the infusion set.
options.C: Recommend she continues using the current infusion set but adds a supplemental injection of long-acting insulin to ensure background coverage.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the clinic. She reports experiencing frequent episodes of hypoglycemia (blood glucose 50-60 mg/dL) in the late afternoon, approximately 3-4 hours after her lunchtime insulin dose. She asks for guidance on how to best manage these predictable hypoglycemic episodes. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial management strategy?

options.A: Advise her to increase her lunchtime carbohydrate intake by 30 grams to prevent the afternoon drop in blood glucose.
options.B: Recommend she reduce her morning long-acting insulin dose by 20% to create a higher baseline glucose level throughout the day.
options.C: Instruct her to treat each episode with 4 ounces of orange juice and then consume a protein-rich snack like cheese or nuts to prevent recurrence.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has gastroenteritis with vomiting. Her blood glucose is 280 mg/dL. When counseling her about fluid intake during illness, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: She should drink only plain water to avoid raising her blood glucose further, as all other fluids contain sugar.
options.B: She should avoid all fluids until her vomiting stops completely to prevent aspiration and worsening nausea.
options.C: She should drink sugary sodas exclusively to provide both hydration and energy since she's unable to eat solid food.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and a GLP-1 receptor agonist develops gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea. She asks about appropriate clear liquid choices to prevent hypoglycemia. Which of the following recommendations is correct regarding gelatin consumption during this illness?

options.A: She should consume only sugar-free gelatin, as regular gelatin contains too much sugar and will cause dangerous hyperglycemia during illness.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin products entirely, as the gelatin protein content can worsen dehydration and ketosis during illness.
options.C: She should consume gelatin with added honey or corn syrup to ensure adequate carbohydrate intake, as this provides the most reliable energy source when sick.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the clinic for routine follow-up. She reports experiencing occasional hypoglycemic episodes (glucose 55-65 mg/dL) at home, typically mid-afternoon. She asks for guidance on the most appropriate initial management step for these symptomatic but mild episodes. What should you advise?

options.A: Take 15 grams of simple carbohydrates (like 4 oz of fruit juice), wait 15 minutes, and if still hypoglycemic, repeat with another 15 grams of carbohydrates.
options.B: Immediately administer 1 mg of glucagon intramuscularly to rapidly reverse the hypoglycemia and prevent progression to severe symptoms.
options.C: Consume a high-protein snack without carbohydrates, such as cheese or nuts, to provide a steady, prolonged source of glucose without causing rebound hyperglycemia.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has developed gastroenteritis with vomiting. She is following her sick-day plan but wants to know when she should contact her doctor immediately. Which of the following situations would warrant an immediate call?

options.A: If her blood glucose readings are consistently above 180 mg/dL for more than 6 hours, regardless of ketone levels or ability to keep fluids down.
options.B: If she needs to skip her rapid-acting insulin doses because she hasn't been able to eat solid food, even if her blood glucose remains elevated.
options.C: If she experiences mild to moderate ketones (trace to small on urine strips) but feels well enough to continue drinking clear fluids at home.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for follow-up. She reports frequent hypoglycemia (3-4 times weekly) in the late afternoon, typically around 4-5 PM. Her current basal rate is set at a constant 0.8 units/hour throughout the day. Her carbohydrate ratio is 1:10 for all meals, and her correction factor is 1:40. She eats lunch at noon and does not typically snack in the afternoon. What is the most appropriate initial adjustment to her insulin pump settings to address this pattern?

options.A: Increase her afternoon basal rate to 1.2 units/hour from 2 PM to 6 PM to better match her insulin needs during that period.
options.B: Switch her to a single, higher constant basal rate of 1.0 units/hour for the entire day to provide more consistent background insulin coverage.
options.C: Discontinue the pump and transition back to multiple daily injections with long-acting insulin, as pumps are clearly causing dangerous hypoglycemia in her case.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and a GLP-1 receptor agonist develops gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea. She asks why her diabetes management plan emphasizes drinking plenty of fluids during this illness. Which of the following best explains the rationale?

options.A: Increased fluid intake helps flush out excess ketones through the urine, which is the primary concern for all sick patients with diabetes regardless of type.
options.B: Drinking extra fluids dilutes the blood, lowering glucose levels directly and reducing the need for medication adjustments during illness.
options.C: The main goal is to prevent hypoglycemia by maintaining blood volume, as dehydration is the leading cause of low blood sugar during gastrointestinal illnesses.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 180-220 mg/dL) despite appropriate basal rate adjustments. She reports her pump is functioning normally, and she has been changing infusion sets every 3 days as recommended. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in troubleshooting?

options.A: Increase all basal rates by 20% immediately and continue this new rate indefinitely, as persistent hyperglycemia indicates chronic insulin resistance requiring higher doses.
options.B: Discontinue the pump and switch back to multiple daily injections for at least one week to 'reset' insulin sensitivity before resuming pump therapy.
options.C: Advise her to administer a correction bolus through the pump every hour until glucose normalizes, as this will overcome any potential insulin absorption issues.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 31 kg/m², no comorbidities) presents for initial management. He is motivated to make lifestyle changes. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial treatment approach?

options.A: Start metformin immediately at maximum dose (2000 mg daily) to achieve rapid glycemic control, then add lifestyle counseling once glucose is normalized.
options.B: Begin with a 6-month trial of diet and exercise alone, delaying all pharmacotherapy regardless of HbA1c response, to assess his commitment to lifestyle changes.
options.C: Initiate insulin therapy upfront to 'rest the pancreas' and preserve beta-cell function, as this provides the most effective long-term glycemic control.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes is concerned about hydration and asks her primary care provider which beverages are both calorie-free and caffeine-free for daily consumption. Which of the following recommendations would be appropriate?

options.A: Diet sodas are an excellent choice because they contain artificial sweeteners that have no effect on blood glucose and are completely calorie-free.
options.B: Fruit-infused water with lemon or cucumber slices is recommended as it provides vitamins without calories or caffeine.
options.C: Clear sports drinks without added sugar are ideal for hydration as they replace electrolytes without affecting blood sugar.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the emergency department with hypoglycemia (glucose 48 mg/dL) and confusion. She is unable to take oral carbohydrates safely. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial management?

options.A: Administer 1 mg of intramuscular glucagon and wait 15 minutes before reassessing glucose levels.
options.B: Start a continuous intravenous infusion of 10% dextrose solution at 100 mL/hour to gradually correct the hypoglycemia.
options.C: Give 15-20 grams of oral glucose gel applied to the buccal mucosa while the patient is still confused.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (blood glucose consistently >250 mg/dL) for the past 24 hours. She reports no recent illness, has been eating her usual diet, and has changed her infusion set twice during this period. Her pump history shows no alarms. What is the most appropriate initial action?

options.A: Immediately increase all basal rates by 50% for the next 24 hours to overcome insulin resistance, then reassess.
options.B: Discontinue the pump and switch to multiple daily injections using her usual insulin doses until her next clinic appointment in 2 weeks.
options.C: Administer a large correction bolus (e.g., 10 units) through the pump and wait 4-6 hours to see if glucose levels improve before investigating further.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. She reports excellent glycemic control with a time-in-range of 85% and an A1c of 6.8%. She mentions she will be traveling to a different time zone (6 hours ahead) for a 10-day vacation. What is the most appropriate guidance regarding her insulin pump settings during travel?

options.A: Advise her to temporarily increase her basal insulin rate by 20% for the entire trip to compensate for potential stress and dietary changes during travel.
options.B: Recommend she switch to multiple daily injections for the duration of the trip, as insulin pumps are too complex to manage across time zones.
options.C: Instruct her to keep the pump on her home time zone settings and manually adjust meal boluses based on local meal times without changing basal rates.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years presents for her annual exam. Her HbA1c has been consistently around 7.2% over the past year. She has no visual symptoms. According to current guidelines, when should she next be scheduled for diabetic retinopathy screening?

options.A: She should be screened immediately and then every 3 months due to her elevated HbA1c level.
options.B: Since she has no visual symptoms, screening can be deferred until she develops blurry vision or floaters.
options.C: She should have screening every 5 years since her diabetes is well-controlled and she's under 50.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) and no cardiovascular disease presents for initial management. He has tried lifestyle modifications for 3 months with minimal improvement. Based on current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start immediate insulin therapy to achieve rapid glycemic control and preserve beta-cell function
options.B: Prescribe sulfonylurea monotherapy as first-line treatment since it's the most cost-effective option
options.C: Begin with DPP-4 inhibitor therapy due to its neutral effect on weight and low hypoglycemia risk, regardless of cardiovascular considerations
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has developed gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea for 12 hours. She is managing at home but is concerned about when she should contact her healthcare provider immediately. Based on standard sick-day management guidelines, which of the following situations warrants immediate contact?

options.A: She should call if her blood glucose levels are consistently above 180 mg/dL, as this indicates impending diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) and requires immediate insulin adjustment.
options.B: She should wait to contact her provider until she has been unable to keep down any food or fluids for at least 24 hours, as shorter periods can usually be managed with over-the-counter medications.
options.C: She should only call if she develops a fever above 101.5°F (38.6°C), as lower temperatures are typical with viral illnesses and don't require medical intervention in diabetes.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the clinic for follow-up. She reports experiencing frequent episodes of hypoglycemia (blood glucose <70 mg/dL) in the late afternoon, often accompanied by symptoms of sweating and shakiness. She asks what the most appropriate initial step should be to address this pattern of recurrent hypoglycemia.

options.A: Increase her basal insulin dose in the morning to prevent hyperglycemia that might be triggering a rebound hypoglycemia later in the day.
options.B: Instruct her to consume 15 grams of complex carbohydrates, like a slice of whole wheat bread, as soon as she feels symptoms, and repeat this every 15 minutes until her symptoms resolve.
options.C: Advise her to check her blood glucose only when she feels symptoms, and if it's low, to treat it with 4 ounces of orange juice and then have a small snack like crackers with peanut butter.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 31 kg/m²) presents for initial management. He has no known cardiovascular disease, normal renal function, and no contraindications to any diabetes medications. Which of the following approaches represents appropriate initial pharmacologic management?

options.A: Start immediate insulin therapy to achieve rapid glycemic control and 'rest the pancreas' for beta-cell recovery.
options.B: Begin with a sulfonylurea as first-line therapy due to its potent glucose-lowering effects and low cost, regardless of cardiovascular risk considerations.
options.C: Prescribe metformin only if lifestyle modifications fail after 6 months, as all patients deserve a prolonged trial of diet and exercise alone first.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes asks about the importance of wearing a medical identification tag. Which of the following statements accurately explains the primary reason for wearing medical identification in diabetes?

options.A: It allows emergency responders to immediately administer a glucose injection if you're found unconscious, preventing hypoglycemic brain damage.
options.B: It provides your exact insulin dose and timing so paramedics can continue your regular regimen during transport to the hospital.
options.C: It ensures that if you have a hyperglycemic emergency, emergency personnel will withhold all fluids to prevent cerebral edema.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) presents for initial management. He has no cardiovascular disease, normal renal function, and is not on any medications. His BMI is 28 kg/m². Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial pharmacological approach?

options.A: Start immediate insulin therapy to achieve rapid glycemic control and preserve beta-cell function.
options.B: Prescribe a sulfonylurea as first-line therapy since it provides the strongest glucose-lowering effect among oral agents.
options.C: Begin metformin only if lifestyle modifications fail to lower HbA1c below 7.0% after 6 months of dedicated effort.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years, well-controlled on metformin with an HbA1c of 6.8%, presents for routine follow-up. She has no visual symptoms and her last dilated eye exam was 18 months ago. According to current guidelines, what is the appropriate recommendation for diabetic retinopathy screening?

options.A: No screening is needed since she has no visual symptoms and her diabetes is well-controlled.
options.B: She should have annual screening with a standard Snellen visual acuity chart in the primary care office.
options.C: She should be referred for a comprehensive dilated eye exam only if her HbA1c rises above 7.5%.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. She reports frequent mild hypoglycemia (glucose 60-70 mg/dL) occurring 2-3 hours after meals, despite appropriate pre-meal boluses. Her current insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio is 1:10, correction factor is 1:50, and basal rate is 0.8 units/hour. Continuous glucose monitoring shows her glucose rises appropriately after meals then declines sharply. Which adjustment would be most appropriate to address this pattern?

options.A: Increase the basal insulin rate by 20% to provide better overall coverage and prevent postprandial drops
options.B: Switch to a fixed pre-meal bolus of 5 units for all meals regardless of carbohydrate content to standardize insulin delivery
options.C: Discontinue the insulin pump and return to multiple daily injections with NPH insulin to provide more predictable insulin action
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years has well-controlled hypertension and an HbA1c of 6.8%. She has no visual symptoms. According to current guidelines, when should she have her next diabetic retinopathy screening?

options.A: She should be screened annually because she has had diabetes for more than 5 years, regardless of her current glycemic control.
options.B: Since she has no visual symptoms and her HbA1c is below 7.0%, screening can be deferred for 3 years.
options.C: She needs immediate referral to an ophthalmologist for a comprehensive dilated eye exam due to her diabetes duration exceeding 5 years.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has gastroenteritis with vomiting. Her blood glucose readings have been elevated (250-320 mg/dL) despite reduced oral intake. When counseling her about fluid management during this illness, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: She should drink only sugar-free fluids to avoid worsening hyperglycemia, even if this means drinking less overall.
options.B: She should limit fluid intake to prevent dilution of her blood and worsening of hyperglycemia, focusing instead on adjusting her insulin doses.
options.C: She should drink large amounts of plain water exclusively, as this will best flush out ketones without affecting blood glucose.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the emergency department with hypoglycemia (glucose 48 mg/dL). She is alert but diaphoretic and anxious. After confirming her glucose level, what is the most appropriate initial management?

options.A: Administer 1 mg of intramuscular glucagon immediately to rapidly reverse hypoglycemia.
options.B: Start a dextrose 5% in water intravenous infusion at 100 mL/hour to prevent rebound hyperglycemia.
options.C: Give 4 ounces of orange juice orally and recheck glucose in 30 minutes.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent fasting hyperglycemia (180-220 mg/dL) despite appropriate basal rate settings. She reports accurate carbohydrate counting and consistent pre-meal boluses. Continuous glucose monitoring shows stable overnight readings but a steady rise beginning around 4 AM. Which of the following adjustments would be most appropriate?

options.A: Increase the basal insulin rate throughout the entire 24-hour period to address the morning hyperglycemia
options.B: Switch to multiple daily injections with long-acting insulin, as pumps are ineffective for dawn phenomenon
options.C: Add a bedtime dose of intermediate-acting NPH insulin while continuing the pump's basal insulin overnight
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the clinic for a routine follow-up. She reports experiencing occasional mild hypoglycemic episodes (glucose 55-65 mg/dL) with symptoms of sweating and shakiness. She asks what she should do when these episodes occur. Based on current guidelines for managing mild hypoglycemia in an alert patient, which of the following is the recommended initial approach?

options.A: Immediately administer 1 mg of intramuscular glucagon to rapidly reverse the hypoglycemia and prevent progression to severe symptoms.
options.B: Drink 8 ounces of diet soda or another sugar-free beverage to provide hydration without raising glucose levels too quickly.
options.C: Take 15 grams of complex carbohydrates like whole wheat bread or brown rice, then wait 45-60 minutes before rechecking glucose.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years presents for her annual diabetes review. Her HbA1c has been stable at 7.2% for the past year. She has no visual symptoms. According to current guidelines, when should she next have diabetic retinopathy screening?

options.A: She should have screening every 3 years since her HbA1c is well-controlled and she has no symptoms.
options.B: She should have screening only when she develops visual symptoms like blurred vision or floaters.
options.C: She should have screening every 5 years because she is under 50 years old and has had diabetes for less than 10 years.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with symptoms of hypoglycemia (sweating, tremor, palpitations). Her blood glucose is 55 mg/dL (3.1 mmol/L). She is conscious and able to swallow safely. According to current guidelines, what is the appropriate initial management?

options.A: Administer 1 mg of glucagon intramuscularly immediately to rapidly correct the hypoglycemia.
options.B: Give 4 ounces of orange juice and recheck blood glucose in 30 minutes, as this allows time for natural stabilization.
options.C: Provide a complex carbohydrate like a whole wheat cracker or half a sandwich to prevent rebound hypoglycemia.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes is concerned about hydration and asks her primary care provider which beverages she can consume freely without affecting her blood glucose. Which of the following recommendations would be appropriate for calorie-free, caffeine-free hydration?

options.A: Diet sodas with artificial sweeteners are completely safe and can be consumed in unlimited quantities as they have no calories and no caffeine.
options.B: Fruit-infused water with lemon or cucumber slices is ideal since the natural sugars from fruits don't count toward calorie intake and won't affect blood sugar.
options.C: Clear sports drinks labeled 'zero sugar' are recommended for optimal electrolyte balance and can replace water for daily hydration needs.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 180-220 mg/dL) despite appropriate basal rate settings. She reports no recent illness, stress, or dietary changes. Her pump reservoir and infusion set were changed yesterday. What is the most appropriate next step in troubleshooting?

options.A: Immediately increase the basal insulin rate by 20% across all time segments and monitor for improvement over the next 48 hours.
options.B: Suspect insulin degradation and advise her to store her insulin vials in the freezer compartment of her refrigerator to preserve potency.
options.C: Assume the hyperglycemia is due to dawn phenomenon and instruct her to program a temporary basal rate increase only from 3 AM to 8 AM.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and glipizide calls the office while experiencing vomiting and diarrhea for the past 12 hours. She asks when she should seek immediate medical attention. Based on standard sick-day management guidelines for diabetes, which of the following situations would warrant contacting her doctor immediately?

options.A: If her blood glucose readings are consistently below 180 mg/dL, as this indicates her medications are working too well and she should stop them temporarily to prevent hypoglycemia.
options.B: If she is unable to keep down solid food for more than 6 hours, she should continue her usual dose of glipizide but skip metformin until she can eat normally again.
options.C: If she develops moderate ketones in her urine (trace to small amount) but feels otherwise fine, she should double her fluid intake and check again in 24 hours before calling.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the clinic for follow-up. She reports experiencing frequent episodes of hypoglycemia (blood glucose <70 mg/dL) in the late afternoon, often accompanied by symptoms of sweating and shakiness. She asks for guidance on how to best manage these episodes when they occur. Based on current evidence-based guidelines, which of the following represents the most appropriate initial management for a conscious patient experiencing symptomatic hypoglycemia?

options.A: Administer 15-20 grams of fast-acting carbohydrate (e.g., 4-6 glucose tablets or 4 oz of fruit juice), recheck blood glucose in 15 minutes, and repeat treatment if still below 70 mg/dL.
options.B: Immediately administer glucagon subcutaneously to rapidly raise blood glucose and prevent potential progression to severe hypoglycemia.
options.C: Consume a large, mixed meal containing protein and fat (e.g., a peanut butter sandwich) to provide sustained glucose elevation and prevent rebound hypoglycemia.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with gastroenteritis and poor oral intake. She asks about using gelatin to help with hydration. Which of the following recommendations about gelatin selection during illness is appropriate?

options.A: She should choose sugar-free gelatin exclusively, as regular gelatin contains too much sugar and will dangerously raise her blood glucose during illness.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin products and instead only consume clear broth, as gelatin provides no meaningful nutritional benefit during illness.
options.C: She should mix her regular gelatin with extra sugar to provide additional calories since she's eating less, and adjust her insulin dose accordingly.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 45-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 31 kg/m²) presents for initial management. He has no cardiovascular disease history, normal renal function, and is asymptomatic. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start immediate insulin therapy to achieve rapid glycemic control and 'rest the pancreas'
options.B: Begin with sulfonylurea monotherapy as first-line treatment, as it's the most cost-effective option
options.C: Prescribe metformin only if lifestyle modifications fail after 6 months of documented non-compliance
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is discussing emergency preparedness with her primary care physician. When asked about the importance of wearing a medical identification tag or carrying a card, which of the following statements would be most appropriate?

options.A: It is primarily to ensure that emergency responders will administer intravenous glucose immediately if you are found unconscious, as hypoglycemia is the only acute complication requiring rapid identification.
options.B: The main purpose is to alert hospital staff to check for diabetes-related organ damage upon arrival, so they can prioritize ordering an echocardiogram and retinal exam during the emergency evaluation.
options.C: It is most important for preventing misdiagnosis; for example, ensuring that symptoms of diabetic ketoacidosis are not mistaken for intoxication, so police don't mistakenly make an arrest.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with symptoms of hypoglycemia (sweating, tremor, palpitations). Her fingerstick glucose is 52 mg/dL (2.9 mmol/L). She is alert and oriented. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial management?

options.A: Administer 15-20 grams of complex carbohydrates like whole wheat bread or oatmeal to provide sustained glucose release.
options.B: Give 1 mg of intramuscular glucagon immediately, as this is the fastest way to raise blood glucose in conscious patients.
options.C: Have her drink 4 ounces of orange juice, then wait 30 minutes before rechecking her glucose to avoid overtreatment.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with gastroenteritis and vomiting. When discussing sick-day management, which of the following statements about fluid intake during illness is correct?

options.A: She should drink only sugar-free fluids to avoid raising her blood glucose, even if this means drinking less overall.
options.B: She should drink large amounts of plain water exclusively, as this is the most effective way to prevent dehydration without affecting blood sugar.
options.C: She should avoid drinking fluids if her blood glucose is above 200 mg/dL, as hydration might worsen hyperglycemia by increasing renal glucose excretion.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old Hispanic man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 31 kg/m², no microvascular complications) presents for initial management. He has no contraindications to metformin. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start insulin glargine immediately to achieve rapid glycemic control and 'rest the pancreas,' then consider oral agents once HbA1c is below 7.0%
options.B: Begin with a sulfonylurea (glipizide) as first-line therapy since it provides stronger glucose-lowering than metformin and addresses insulin deficiency
options.C: Prescribe a GLP-1 receptor agonist first due to his BMI >30 kg/m², bypassing metformin to prioritize weight loss benefits
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. Her time-in-range (TIR) over the past 14 days is 65%, with frequent postprandial hyperglycemia. Her current carbohydrate ratio is 1 unit per 15 grams. Which of the following would be an appropriate initial adjustment to improve her postprandial glucose control?

options.A: Increase the basal insulin rate by 20% across all time segments to provide more background coverage for meals.
options.B: Switch to a fixed pre-meal bolus of 5 units before each main meal, regardless of carbohydrate intake.
