options.A: I immediately drink a glass of orange juice and then eat a small bag of potato chips to prevent my sugar from dropping again.
options.B: I take 4 glucose tablets right away, then wait 15 minutes and check my blood sugar. If it's still low, I repeat with another 4 tablets.
options.C: I eat a tablespoon of peanut butter first because the fat helps stabilize my blood sugar, then follow with a piece of fruit if I'm still feeling shaky.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years presents for routine follow-up. Her HbA1c has been 7.2-7.8% over the past year. She has no visual symptoms. According to current guidelines, when should she be referred for her first diabetic retinopathy screening?

options.A: Immediately, because her HbA1c is above 7.0%, indicating high risk for vision-threatening retinopathy
options.B: Only when she develops visual symptoms such as blurry vision or floaters, as screening is unnecessary in asymptomatic patients
options.C: At age 50, since screening should begin 10 years after diabetes diagnosis or at age 50, whichever comes first
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. She reports occasional unexplained hyperglycemia despite consistent carbohydrate counting. Her pump data shows basal rates are stable, and she has no symptoms of illness. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial approach to investigate these episodes?

options.A: Immediately increase all basal insulin rates by 20% to create a safety buffer against hyperglycemia, as this is the most common cause of unexplained high glucose readings.
options.B: Advise her to stop using the pump's temporary basal rate feature entirely, as this technology is known to cause unpredictable glucose fluctuations in all users.
options.C: Recommend she replace her insulin pump infusion set only when it visibly malfunctions or causes pain, since frequent changes increase infection risk and are unnecessary for most patients.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and a GLP-1 receptor agonist develops gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea. She asks about appropriate gelatin consumption during her illness to maintain hydration. Which of the following recommendations is correct?

options.A: She should consume only sugar-free gelatin, as regular gelatin contains too much sugar and will cause dangerous hyperglycemia during illness.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin products and instead drink only plain water, since gelatin contains carbohydrates that could interfere with her diabetes management during sickness.
options.C: She should consume gelatin with added electrolytes and extra sugar to provide both hydration and an energy boost, as her body needs extra calories when fighting infection.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) and no cardiovascular disease presents for initial management. He has a BMI of 31 kg/m² and reports no contraindications to any diabetes medications. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start immediate insulin therapy to achieve rapid glycemic control and preserve beta-cell function.
options.B: Prescribe a sulfonylurea as first-line therapy due to its proven efficacy in lowering glucose and low cost.
options.C: Initiate metformin only if lifestyle modifications fail to lower HbA1c below 7.0% after 6 months.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. She reports excellent glycemic control with time-in-range consistently above 85% and no hypoglycemia. During the visit, she mentions she will be traveling across multiple time zones for a two-week vacation. What is the most appropriate initial recommendation regarding her insulin pump management for this travel?

options.A: Advise her to temporarily switch back to multiple daily injections for the duration of the trip, as insulin pumps are too complex to manage during international travel.
options.B: Recommend she set her pump to a single, fixed basal rate throughout the entire trip to avoid confusion with time zone changes.
options.C: Instruct her to keep her pump on her home time zone and manually adjust all meal boluses based on local meal times without changing pump settings.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. She reports occasional unexplained hyperglycemia (250-300 mg/dL) that doesn't respond well to correction boluses. Her pump data shows consistent basal insulin delivery. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in troubleshooting?

options.A: Immediately increase the basal rate by 20% across all time segments to provide more background insulin coverage.
options.B: Switch to multiple daily injections temporarily since pump therapy is clearly failing, then reconsider pump use after 2 weeks.
options.C: Advise her to take extra correction boluses manually every hour until glucose normalizes, regardless of insulin-on-board calculations.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with symptoms of hypoglycemia (sweating, palpitations, confusion) and a measured blood glucose of 52 mg/dL. She is conscious and able to swallow safely. According to current guidelines, what is the appropriate initial management?

options.A: Administer 15 grams of glucose tablets, then wait 30 minutes before rechecking blood glucose, regardless of symptom resolution.
options.B: Give 4 ounces of orange juice followed by a protein-rich snack like cheese or nuts to prevent rebound hypoglycemia.
options.C: Immediately administer 1 mg of intramuscular glucagon, as this is the fastest way to raise blood glucose in conscious patients.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes develops gastroenteritis with vomiting. Her sick-day management plan emphasizes adequate fluid intake. Which of the following best explains the primary rationale for this recommendation?

options.A: Drinking large volumes of plain water helps flush excess ketones from the bloodstream through increased urine output, directly correcting diabetic ketoacidosis.
options.B: Extra fluids are needed primarily to dilute high blood sugar levels; therefore, drinking sugar-free liquids exclusively is essential to avoid worsening hyperglycemia.
options.C: Increased fluid intake prevents dehydration, which can cause blood glucose levels to drop dangerously low, so it's mainly a strategy to avoid hypoglycemia.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years has well-controlled blood glucose (HbA1c 6.8%) and no visual symptoms. She asks about diabetic retinopathy screening. Which of the following statements about her screening is correct?

options.A: Since her diabetes is well-controlled and she has no symptoms, she can safely delay screening until she reaches age 50.
options.B: She should perform monthly self-exams using an Amsler grid at home, and only needs formal screening if she notices any distortion.
options.C: A comprehensive dilated eye exam by an optometrist every two years is sufficient for her screening needs.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and a GLP-1 receptor agonist develops gastroenteritis with vomiting. She asks about appropriate gelatin consumption during her illness. Based on current diabetes sick-day management guidelines, which recommendation is correct?

options.A: She should choose sugar-free gelatin exclusively, as any sugar intake during illness will dangerously elevate her blood glucose and should be avoided.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin products and instead consume only clear broth, as gelatin contains carbohydrates that could interfere with her medication absorption during vomiting episodes.
options.C: She should select gelatin with added electrolytes and high sugar content to provide maximal calories and prevent hypoglycemia, since she is at high risk for low blood sugar while vomiting.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 31 kg/m², no cardiovascular disease or kidney impairment) presents for initial treatment planning. He is motivated to improve his health. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start immediate insulin therapy to achieve rapid glycemic control and 'rest the pancreas' for better long-term function
options.B: Begin with sulfonylurea monotherapy as first-line treatment since it's inexpensive and provides strong glucose-lowering effects
options.C: Prescribe metformin only if lifestyle modifications fail after 6 months, as medication should be a last resort in newly diagnosed patients
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. She reports frequent mild hypoglycemia (glucose 60-70 mg/dL) occurring 2-3 hours after meals, despite appropriate pre-meal boluses. Her current pump settings include a basal rate of 0.8 units/hour and a carbohydrate ratio of 1:12. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial adjustment to address this pattern?

options.A: Increase the basal insulin rate by 0.2 units/hour to provide more background insulin coverage throughout the day.
options.B: Switch to a fixed carbohydrate ratio of 1:10 for all meals to reduce postprandial glucose spikes that might be triggering reactive hypoglycemia.
options.C: Add a correction factor of 1:50 to administer additional insulin when glucose exceeds 150 mg/dL after meals.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has developed gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea. She is following her sick-day plan but is unsure when to contact her doctor immediately. Based on standard diabetes sick-day management guidelines, which of the following situations warrants immediate medical contact?

options.A: If her blood glucose readings are consistently above 180 mg/dL for more than 6 hours, even if she is able to keep down fluids and take her insulin as prescribed.
options.B: If she develops mild ketonuria (trace to small on urine ketone strips) but her blood glucose is below 250 mg/dL and she feels alert.
options.C: If she needs to temporarily reduce her basal insulin dose by 20% because she is eating less due to nausea, regardless of her blood glucose levels.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years, well-controlled on metformin (HbA1c 6.8%), presents for her annual exam. She has no visual symptoms. According to current guidelines, what is the appropriate recommendation for diabetic retinopathy screening?

options.A: She should have annual dilated fundus exams by an ophthalmologist starting from the time of her diabetes diagnosis.
options.B: Since she has no visual symptoms and her diabetes is well-controlled, she can wait 2-3 years before her next screening.
options.C: She should perform monthly self-exams using an Amsler grid at home, and only seek professional screening if she notices changes.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (blood glucose consistently >250 mg/dL) for the past 24 hours. She reports no recent illness, has been eating her usual diet, and has been physically active as usual. She has checked her pump and reservoir, and both appear functional. What is the most appropriate initial step in troubleshooting this persistent hyperglycemia?

options.A: Immediately increase all basal insulin rates by 20% and program temporary basal rates for the next 48 hours to correct the hyperglycemia.
options.B: Administer a subcutaneous injection of long-acting insulin while continuing the pump therapy to ensure background insulin coverage.
options.C: Disconnect the pump and take a 'pump holiday' for 24 hours while monitoring blood glucose, assuming the pump settings are causing the problem.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes, managed with metformin and a GLP-1 receptor agonist, develops gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea. She is trying to maintain hydration and asks about consuming gelatin while ill. Which of the following recommendations about gelatin selection during this acute illness is most appropriate?

options.A: She should choose sugar-free gelatin exclusively, as it contains no carbohydrates and will not affect her blood glucose, which is the primary concern during illness.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin products and only consume clear broths, as gelatin is derived from animal collagen and can interfere with her diabetes medications.
options.C: She should choose regular gelatin with sugar, as the glucose is necessary to prevent hypoglycemia during illness, and she can simply increase her insulin dose (if she were on insulin) to compensate.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is discussing emergency preparedness with her primary care provider. Which of the following statements accurately describes the primary clinical rationale for consistently wearing medical identification jewelry or carrying a medical alert card?

options.A: It allows emergency responders to immediately administer a standard 50% dextrose IV bolus for any altered mental status, preventing the need for fingerstick glucose testing.
options.B: It serves as a legal document authorizing bystanders to administer glucagon from the patient's personal supply without fear of liability, even if the patient is conscious.
options.C: Its primary purpose is to alert emergency personnel to check for diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) first in any trauma situation, as DKA is the most common life-threatening emergency in all diabetics.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the clinic for follow-up. She reports experiencing frequent hypoglycemic episodes (blood glucose <70 mg/dL) in the late afternoon, approximately 3-4 hours after her lunchtime insulin dose. She asks for guidance on the most appropriate immediate management strategy to prevent these recurrent episodes. Which of the following would you recommend?

options.A: Increase her lunchtime rapid-acting insulin dose to achieve tighter postprandial control, as this will prevent the glucose from dropping later.
options.B: Add a complex carbohydrate snack, like a granola bar, 2 hours after lunch to provide a sustained glucose release and prevent the drop.
options.C: Switch her lunchtime insulin from a rapid-acting analog to regular human insulin, as its longer duration of action will provide more stable coverage.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. She reports excellent glycemic control with an A1c of 6.8% and time-in-range of 78%. She asks about the recommended frequency for changing her insulin pump infusion set. Which of the following is the appropriate guidance?

options.A: Infusion sets should be changed every 7 days regardless of symptoms, as this matches the typical insulin reservoir capacity and minimizes waste.
options.B: She should change the infusion set only when she notices hyperglycemia or ketones, as changing too frequently increases the risk of lipohypertrophy at insertion sites.
options.C: Since she has excellent control, she can extend infusion set use to 5-6 days to reduce costs, as the risk of infection is minimal with good personal hygiene.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is discussing emergency preparedness with her primary care provider. Which of the following statements accurately describes the primary purpose of wearing a medical identification tag for her condition?

options.A: It allows emergency responders to immediately administer a standard dose of insulin without needing to check blood glucose levels first.
options.B: It serves as a legal document that authorizes bystanders to provide medical treatment without liability concerns.
options.C: It primarily functions to alert others that the individual should receive oral glucose tablets immediately in any emergency situation.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes is concerned about hydration and asks her primary care provider for examples of calorie-free, caffeine-free liquids she can consume throughout the day. Which of the following recommendations would be appropriate?

options.A: Diet sodas are an excellent choice because they contain artificial sweeteners that don't affect blood sugar and provide hydration without calories.
options.B: Fruit-infused water with lemon or cucumber slices is completely calorie-free and won't impact glucose levels since the flavor comes from natural sources.
options.C: Clear sports drinks labeled 'zero sugar' are ideal as they provide electrolytes without calories or caffeine, making them superior to plain water.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and glipizide calls the clinic while experiencing vomiting and diarrhea for the past 12 hours. According to standard sick-day management guidelines, which of the following situations would warrant her contacting her doctor immediately?

options.A: If her blood glucose readings are consistently below 180 mg/dL, as this indicates her medications are working properly and she can continue her usual regimen without adjustment.
options.B: If she is unable to keep down solid food, she should stop all her diabetes medications until she can eat normally again to prevent hypoglycemia.
options.C: If she develops moderate ketones in her urine but feels well enough to drink fluids, she can monitor at home and only call if symptoms worsen over the next 24-48 hours.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%) presents for initial management. He has no cardiovascular disease history, normal renal function, and is not on any medications. His BMI is 28 kg/m². According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start metformin 500 mg daily and titrate to 2000 mg daily over 4 weeks, while simultaneously initiating insulin glargine 10 units at bedtime to achieve rapid glycemic control.
options.B: Prescribe a sulfonylurea (glipizide) as first-line therapy because it provides the most potent glucose-lowering effect and is most effective for initial HbA1c reduction.
options.C: Begin a GLP-1 receptor agonist as monotherapy since it provides both glycemic control and weight loss benefits, making it the optimal first choice for all new type 2 diabetes patients.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%) and no other significant medical history presents for initial management. She has a BMI of 31 kg/m² and expresses strong motivation to avoid medications. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial treatment approach?

options.A: Start metformin immediately at maximum dose (2000 mg daily) to achieve rapid glycemic control, as early aggressive treatment prevents all future complications.
options.B: Prescribe a strict very-low-carbohydrate diet (<30g daily) with no medication, as this alone can reverse diabetes permanently in all patients.
options.C: Delay medication initiation for 6 months while focusing solely on lifestyle changes, since all patients deserve a 'medication-free trial period' regardless of baseline HbA1c.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (blood glucose consistently >250 mg/dL) for the past 8 hours despite multiple correction boluses. She reports no recent illness, stress, or dietary changes. What is the most appropriate initial step in troubleshooting this situation?

options.A: Immediately increase all basal rates by 20% for the next 24 hours to compensate for the apparent insulin resistance
options.B: Disconnect the pump and administer a subcutaneous injection of long-acting insulin, then reconnect the pump after 2 hours
options.C: Assume the insulin has degraded and continue using the current reservoir while adding an oral sulfonylurea medication temporarily
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) and no cardiovascular disease presents for initial management. He has a BMI of 31 kg/m² and reports trying to follow a healthy diet. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start immediate insulin therapy to achieve rapid glycemic control and preserve beta-cell function
options.B: Begin with a sulfonylurea like glipizide as first-line therapy due to its proven efficacy and low cost
options.C: Prescribe metformin only if lifestyle modifications fail after 6 months, as medication should be a last resort
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and a long-acting insulin calls the clinic for advice while experiencing a viral gastroenteritis illness with vomiting. She reports her blood glucose readings have been between 180-220 mg/dL for the past 8 hours. According to standard sick-day management guidelines, which of the following situations would warrant her to contact her doctor immediately?

options.A: If she stops taking her metformin and insulin until she feels better, to avoid hypoglycemia while she is not eating normally.
options.B: If her blood glucose drops below her usual target range to 90 mg/dL, indicating her illness is improving and she can resume her normal medication schedule.
options.C: If she is unable to keep down any fluids or food for more than 4 hours, but her blood glucose remains stable around 200 mg/dL.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the clinic for routine follow-up. She reports experiencing occasional hypoglycemic episodes, typically in the late afternoon. She asks about the most appropriate immediate treatment for symptomatic hypoglycemia (blood glucose 55 mg/dL) when she is at home. Which of the following would you recommend?

options.A: Drink 8 ounces of orange juice and then eat a small piece of cheese to prevent rebound hyperglycemia.
options.B: Take 15 grams of glucose tablets, wait 20 minutes, and if still hypoglycemic, administer a glucagon injection.
options.C: Eat a tablespoon of peanut butter or a handful of nuts, as the fat and protein will provide sustained glucose correction.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%) presents for initial management. He has no cardiovascular disease, normal renal function, and no contraindications to metformin. According to current guidelines, what is the appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start insulin therapy immediately to achieve rapid glycemic control and preserve beta-cell function
options.B: Begin with a sulfonylurea like glipizide as first-line therapy since it provides stronger glucose-lowering than metformin
options.C: Prescribe a GLP-1 receptor agonist as initial monotherapy due to its cardiovascular benefits, even without established cardiovascular disease
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with gastroenteritis and mild hyperglycemia. When counseling her about fluid management during illness, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: She should drink only sugar-free fluids to avoid worsening hyperglycemia, even if this means drinking less overall.
options.B: She should restrict fluids to prevent dilutional hyponatremia, as this is a common complication of gastroenteritis in diabetes.
options.C: She should drink large amounts of plain water exclusively, as this is the safest way to prevent dehydration without affecting blood glucose.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents with persistent hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 180-220 mg/dL) for the past week. She reports no recent illness, changes in diet, or physical activity. Her pump history shows consistent basal insulin delivery. What is the most appropriate initial step in troubleshooting this hyperglycemia?

options.A: Immediately increase the basal insulin rate by 20% across all time segments to correct the pattern, as this is likely due to insulin resistance progression.
options.B: Disconnect the pump and administer a subcutaneous injection of long-acting insulin, then resume pump therapy after 24 hours to 'reset' insulin sensitivity.
options.C: Assume the hyperglycemia is due to infusion set failure and change both the infusion set and insulin reservoir, but continue using the same insulin vial to avoid waste.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%) and no other comorbidities presents for initial management. He has a BMI of 31 kg/m² and reports trying lifestyle modifications for 3 months with minimal improvement. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic approach?

options.A: Start insulin therapy immediately to achieve rapid glycemic control and prevent complications.
options.B: Begin with sulfonylurea monotherapy as first-line treatment due to its low cost and proven efficacy.
options.C: Prescribe metformin only if lifestyle changes fail completely, and wait 6 more months before initiating any medication.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using an insulin pump presents for routine follow-up. She reports generally good glycemic control but mentions occasional unexplained hyperglycemia. When reviewing her pump settings, which of the following approaches would be appropriate for evaluating potential insulin resistance?

options.A: Increase the basal insulin rate by 20% across all time segments for one week, then adjust based on fasting glucose patterns.
options.B: Switch from rapid-acting insulin analogs to regular human insulin in the pump to better match delayed carbohydrate absorption.
options.C: Discontinue the insulin pump temporarily and return to multiple daily injections with NPH insulin to assess true basal needs.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the clinic for follow-up. She reports experiencing frequent episodes of hypoglycemia (blood glucose <70 mg/dL) in the late afternoon, approximately 3-4 hours after lunch. She is on a basal-bolus insulin regimen. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial adjustment to reduce these recurrent hypoglycemic episodes?

options.A: Increase her lunchtime rapid-acting insulin dose to prevent postprandial hyperglycemia that might be triggering a reactive drop later.
options.B: Advise her to consume a high-protein snack without carbohydrates immediately when her glucose drops below 70 mg/dL to provide sustained energy.
options.C: Switch her afternoon rapid-acting insulin from a modern analog to regular human insulin to prolong its action and cover the period better.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes is concerned about hydration during a heat wave. She asks her primary care provider which calorie-free, caffeine-free beverages are safe for her to consume regularly. Which of the following recommendations would be appropriate?

options.A: Diet sodas containing artificial sweeteners like aspartame are ideal because they have no calories and help satisfy sweet cravings without affecting blood sugar.
options.B: Fruit-infused water with lemon or cucumber slices is recommended, as the natural sugars from the fruit are minimal and won't impact glucose levels.
options.C: Clear sports drinks labeled 'zero sugar' are excellent options since they provide electrolytes without calories or caffeine.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.8%) presents for initial management. She has no cardiovascular disease, normal renal function, and is not pregnant. Her BMI is 31 kg/m². Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial pharmacological treatment approach?

options.A: Start insulin therapy immediately to achieve rapid glycemic control and preserve beta-cell function.
options.B: Begin with sulfonylurea monotherapy as first-line treatment due to its potent glucose-lowering effects.
options.C: Prescribe metformin only if lifestyle modifications fail after 6 months of intensive dietary counseling.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with gastroenteritis. When discussing sick-day management, why is adequate fluid intake particularly emphasized?

options.A: To dilute excess ketones in the bloodstream by promoting their excretion through increased urine output, which is the primary mechanism for resolving diabetic ketoacidosis.
options.B: Because dehydration directly causes hyperglycemia by increasing cortisol and adrenaline secretion, so drinking fluids will immediately lower blood glucose levels.
options.C: To prevent hypoglycemia during illness by maintaining blood volume, as dehydration is the main cause of low blood sugar when patients are sick.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has developed gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea. She is concerned about when she should contact her healthcare provider immediately. Based on standard sick-day management guidelines, which of the following situations would warrant immediate contact?

options.A: If her blood glucose levels are consistently below 180 mg/dL, as this indicates she is not eating enough and needs insulin adjustment.
options.B: If she is unable to keep down solid food for more than 6 hours, she should stop all insulin until she can eat normally again.
options.C: If she develops moderate ketones in her urine but her blood glucose is only 150 mg/dL, she can safely manage this at home with increased fluid intake alone.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes managed with metformin and a GLP-1 receptor agonist develops gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhea. When counseling her about fluid management during this illness, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: She should drink only small sips of water to avoid worsening nausea, and can safely skip fluids for 6-8 hours if vomiting continues.
options.B: She should switch to drinking only sugar-free electrolyte solutions, as these will prevent both dehydration and hyperglycemia without requiring insulin adjustment.
options.C: She should drink large amounts of plain water exclusively, as this is the safest way to flush out toxins and prevent diabetic ketoacidosis.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents to the clinic for follow-up. She reports experiencing frequent hypoglycemic episodes (blood glucose <70 mg/dL) in the late afternoon, approximately 3-4 hours after her lunchtime insulin dose. She asks what the most appropriate initial step should be to address this pattern of hypoglycemia.

options.A: Increase her lunchtime carbohydrate intake by 30 grams to provide a 'buffer' against the insulin's effect, without adjusting the insulin dose.
options.B: Switch her rapid-acting insulin analog at lunch to regular human insulin, as its longer duration of action will better match the post-meal glucose rise and prevent the late drop.
options.C: Instruct her to treat the hypoglycemia when it occurs with a complex carbohydrate like whole-grain bread, as this will provide a more sustained rise in blood glucose than simple sugars.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes presents with gastroenteritis and poor oral intake. She asks what type of gelatin she should consume to help maintain hydration and prevent hypoglycemia. Which of the following recommendations is appropriate?

options.A: She should choose sugar-free gelatin exclusively, as regular gelatin contains too much sugar and will cause dangerous hyperglycemia during illness.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin products and instead only consume clear broth, as gelatin provides no meaningful nutritional benefit during sickness.
options.C: She should mix her regular gelatin with extra sugar to ensure she gets enough carbohydrates to prevent hypoglycemia, regardless of her current blood glucose level.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes is concerned about hydration and asks which beverages she can consume freely throughout the day without affecting her blood glucose. She specifically requests options that are calorie-free and caffeine-free. Which of the following would be appropriate recommendations?

options.A: Diet sodas with artificial sweeteners are ideal because they contain no calories or caffeine and have been proven to improve insulin sensitivity.
options.B: Fruit-infused water with lemon or cucumber slices is recommended as it provides essential vitamins without calories, though it should be limited to 2 glasses daily to avoid fructose absorption.
options.C: Clear sports drinks labeled 'zero sugar' are excellent for hydration as they replace electrolytes lost through urination, which is common in diabetes, and contain no caffeine.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes asks about the importance of wearing a medical identification bracelet. Which of the following statements accurately describes the primary clinical rationale for this recommendation?

options.A: It allows emergency personnel to immediately administer a glucose tablet or sugary drink if the patient is found unconscious, preventing hypoglycemia.
options.B: It ensures that in an emergency, healthcare providers will prioritize administering insulin first to prevent diabetic ketoacidosis, regardless of the presenting symptoms.
options.C: It provides legal protection by documenting the patient's condition, which prevents medical liability for any treatment decisions made during an emergency.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes, managed with metformin and a GLP-1 receptor agonist, presents with gastroenteritis and poor oral intake. She asks about appropriate gelatin consumption during her illness. Which of the following recommendations is correct?

options.A: She should consume only sugar-free gelatin, as regular gelatin contains too much sugar that will dangerously raise her blood glucose during illness.
options.B: She should avoid all gelatin products and instead consume only clear broth, as gelatin provides no meaningful nutritional value and may interfere with her diabetes medications.
options.C: She should consume gelatin sweetened with honey or agave syrup, as these natural sweeteners are healthier alternatives that won't affect blood glucose levels during illness.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is discussing emergency preparedness with her primary care physician. When asked about the primary purpose of wearing a medical identification bracelet, which of the following statements would be most accurate?

options.A: It allows emergency personnel to immediately administer insulin without checking blood glucose levels, as hypoglycemia is always the most likely emergency in diabetes.
options.B: It serves as a legal document that authorizes any bystander to administer glucagon or insulin found in the patient's belongings without liability concerns.
options.C: It primarily functions to alert others that the patient should receive sugar-free foods and drinks only during any medical emergency, regardless of symptoms.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%, BMI 31 kg/m², no comorbidities) presents for initial management. He is motivated to improve his health. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate initial treatment approach?

options.A: Start metformin immediately and advise him to follow a strict low-carbohydrate diet (<30g carbs daily) to achieve rapid weight loss and glycemic control.
options.B: Prescribe insulin therapy upfront since his HbA1c is above 8.0%, as this provides the fastest glucose control and may preserve beta-cell function.
options.C: Begin with a 3-month trial of lifestyle modification alone, including dietary changes and exercise, before considering any medication regardless of his initial HbA1c level.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents to the podiatry clinic with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot. On examination, there is a deep ulcer extending to the bone, with surrounding erythema and purulent drainage. There is no palpable dorsalis pedis pulse, but capillary refill is <3 seconds. The podiatrist documents this as 'Wagner grade 3.' Which of the following statements about the Wagner classification system for diabetic foot ulcers is correct?

options.A: Wagner grade 3 specifically indicates osteomyelitis, so bone biopsy and culture should be deferred until after a full course of empiric IV antibiotics is completed.
options.B: The Wagner system is primarily based on the depth of tissue involvement and the presence of gangrene, so vascular assessment and neuropathy screening are not required for initial staging.
options.C: A Wagner grade 3 ulcer with exposed bone and signs of infection should be managed with daily wet-to-dry dressing changes alone, as surgical debridement increases the risk of spreading the infection.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents with worsening nausea, early satiety, and postprandial vomiting for 3 months. A gastric emptying study confirms severe diabetic gastroparesis. Her current medications include metformin, glargine insulin, and liraglutide. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic adjustment?

options.A: Increase the liraglutide dose to improve glycemic control, as better glucose management will resolve the gastroparesis symptoms.
options.B: Switch liraglutide to a sulfonylurea like glipizide, as this class does not affect gastric motility and will maintain glucose control.
options.C: Continue liraglutide but add metoclopramide for symptomatic relief, as GLP-1 agonists are essential for cardiovascular protection in all patients with diabetes.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and severe gastroparesis presents for diabetes management review. Her current HbA1c is 8.5%. She is experiencing significant postprandial nausea and early satiety. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in her pharmacologic management?

options.A: Initiate a GLP-1 receptor agonist to improve glycemic control and promote weight loss, as these agents are first-line for patients with gastroparesis.
options.B: Prescribe metformin extended-release to improve insulin sensitivity, as it is the safest option for gastroparesis due to its minimal gastrointestinal side effects.
options.C: Add acarbose to specifically target postprandial hyperglycemia, as its mechanism of action in the gut makes it ideal for gastroparesis management.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing glucose values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick meter readings for the past 24 hours. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) two days ago for immune support. Which of the following represents the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to discontinue her CGM use immediately and switch back to fingerstick monitoring only, as vitamin C permanently damages the sensor's electrochemical components.
options.B: Tell her to double her vitamin C dose to 2000 mg twice daily, as this will help stabilize the sensor readings by saturating the interference effect.
options.C: Recommend she calibrate her CGM more frequently (every 2-3 hours) using her fingerstick readings to 'train' the sensor to correct for the vitamin C interference.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents with a warm, swollen, erythematous left foot. He denies trauma or pain, and his temperature is normal. X-ray shows midfoot fragmentation and dislocation. What is the most appropriate initial management step?

options.A: Immediately start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed osteomyelitis, as the erythema and swelling indicate infection.
options.B: Apply a compression bandage and advise weight-bearing as tolerated to maintain mobility, as the lack of pain suggests a minor soft tissue injury.
options.C: Perform an urgent surgical debridement and drainage procedure due to the radiographic findings of bone destruction.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes and peripheral neuropathy presents to the emergency department with a warm, erythematous, and swollen left foot. He reports no recent trauma or fever. On examination, the foot is significantly swollen with bounding pulses, and the patient reports minimal pain despite the dramatic appearance. Plain radiographs show midfoot fragmentation and joint subluxation. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial management step?

options.A: Immediate surgical debridement and drainage should be performed due to the high suspicion for a deep space infection requiring urgent source control.
options.B: Start high-dose intravenous corticosteroids to reduce the inflammatory response and swelling, as this is likely an acute gout flare in a diabetic patient.
options.C: Apply a compression bandage and advise the patient to continue weight-bearing as tolerated to maintain joint mobility and prevent stiffness.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a real-time continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing glucose values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick readings for the past two days. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) for immune support three days ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to stop the CGM entirely and rely solely on fingerstick testing while taking vitamin C, as the supplement completely invalidates all CGM readings.
options.B: Tell her to double her vitamin C dose to 2000 mg twice daily, as higher antioxidant levels will eventually calibrate the sensor to read more accurately over time.
options.C: Recommend she continue the vitamin C and simply subtract a fixed 50 mg/dL from all CGM readings as a personal correction factor for the duration of sensor wear.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several well-demarcated, atrophic, yellowish-brown plaques with telangiectasias on her shins, diagnosed as necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum. She is concerned about the appearance and asks about management. Which of the following represents appropriate initial management?

options.A: Initiate high-dose systemic corticosteroids immediately to reduce inflammation and prevent ulceration, as this is the most effective first-line treatment.
options.B: Recommend complete bed rest and elevation of the legs at all times to reduce blood flow to the lesions and promote healing.
options.C: Advise that aggressive glycemic control with an A1C target below 5.5% will reliably lead to complete resolution of the existing lesions within 3-6 months.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) has a fasting glucose of 105 mg/dL and postprandial values consistently above 140 mg/dL despite dietary modification. Her BMI is 29 kg/m². She asks about medication options, specifically mentioning metformin. Which of the following statements regarding metformin use in her pregnancy is correct?

options.A: Metformin is absolutely contraindicated in all trimesters of pregnancy due to proven teratogenic effects, and insulin should be the exclusive first-line pharmacological therapy.
options.B: Metformin is completely safe and is the preferred first-line agent over insulin because it does not cross the placenta and has no effect on fetal growth or neonatal outcomes.
options.C: Metformin can be used, but only in the third trimester after organogenesis is complete, and it must be discontinued at 36 weeks to prevent neonatal lactic acidosis during delivery.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her obstetrician is considering pharmacotherapy. Which of the following statements regarding metformin use in this pregnancy is correct?

options.A: Metformin should be avoided entirely in pregnancy as it is FDA Pregnancy Category D and known to cause major congenital malformations when used in the first trimester.
options.B: Metformin is the preferred first-line pharmacologic agent for GDM because it does not cross the placenta and therefore poses no risk to the fetus throughout pregnancy.
options.C: Metformin may be used cautiously in pregnancy, but it must be discontinued by 36 weeks gestation to prevent neonatal lactic acidosis and must never be used during labor or delivery.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents with a fever and body aches. She asks if she can safely take acetaminophen for symptom relief while wearing her CGM. Which of the following statements about acetaminophen and CGM accuracy is correct?

options.A: Acetaminophen causes significant interference with all CGM systems, so she should switch to ibuprofen which has no effect on sensor readings.
options.B: Only high-dose intravenous acetaminophen affects CGM readings; standard oral doses under 1,000 mg are completely safe for all sensors.
options.C: The interference is predictable - acetaminophen always causes falsely low CGM readings, so she should add 20% to all displayed values while taking it.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a Dexcom G7 continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents with a headache. She asks what over-the-counter pain medication she can take without affecting her CGM readings. Which of the following is the most appropriate advice regarding acetaminophen interference?

options.A: Take acetaminophen as needed; modern CGMs like the G7 are completely immune to acetaminophen interference due to advanced enzyme technology.
options.B: Avoid acetaminophen entirely and use ibuprofen instead, as all NSAIDs are safe for CGM users and won't cause false readings.
options.C: You can take acetaminophen safely if you stay under 2,000 mg per day, as interference only occurs at higher overdose-level doses.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 20 years presents with several well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on her anterior shins, consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum (NLD). She is concerned about the appearance and occasional ulceration. Which of the following represents an appropriate management approach?

options.A: Initiate high-potency topical corticosteroids with occlusion and advise her that this will completely resolve the lesions if her HbA1c is maintained below 6.5%.
options.B: Recommend immediate surgical excision of all plaques to prevent malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma, which is a common complication of long-standing NLD.
options.C: Prescribe a course of oral tetracycline antibiotics, as NLD is primarily caused by a chronic bacterial infection of the dermis that requires systemic antimicrobial therapy.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old pregnant patient at 28 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) is starting continuous glucose monitoring (CGM). She asks what her daily time-in-range (TIR) target should be. Based on current international consensus guidelines for GDM, what is the recommended TIR target?

options.A: The target should be the same as for type 1 diabetes in pregnancy: >70% of readings between 63-140 mg/dL (3.5-7.8 mmol/L).
options.B: Since pregnancy requires tighter control, aim for >90% of readings between 70-120 mg/dL (3.9-6.7 mmol/L) with minimal time below range.
options.C: Given the transient nature of GDM, a more lenient target of >50% of readings between 70-180 mg/dL (3.9-10.0 mmol/L) is sufficient to prevent macrosomia.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) for self-management. During her clinic visit, she asks what her primary daily glucose target should be. Based on current international consensus guidelines for GDM, which of the following represents the correct Time in Range (TIR) target for her condition?

options.A: The primary goal should be to achieve a Time in Range (70-180 mg/dL) of >90%, as this is the same target used for non-pregnant adults with type 2 diabetes.
options.B: She should aim for a Time in Range of 63-140 mg/dL for >90% of the day, with less than 1% of readings below 63 mg/dL, matching the stricter targets used in type 1 diabetes pregnancy.
options.C: Since her GDM is diet-controlled, formal Time in Range targets are not necessary; she should simply focus on keeping all fasting values under 95 mg/dL and postprandial values under 140 mg/dL without specific duration goals.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents to the clinic with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot. On examination, there is a deep ulcer extending to the bone, with surrounding erythema and purulent drainage. There is no gangrene. According to the Wagner classification system for diabetic foot ulcers, what is the grade of this ulcer?

options.A: Grade 1, because the ulcer is superficial and involves only the epidermis and dermis.
options.B: Grade 3, because the presence of purulent drainage and erythema indicates a deep abscess requiring surgical drainage.
options.C: Grade 4, because the ulcer extends to bone and there is localized gangrene of the forefoot.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot. On examination, there is a deep ulcer extending to the bone, with surrounding erythema and purulent drainage. There is no gangrene. According to the Wagner classification system for diabetic foot ulcers, what is the grade of this ulcer?

options.A: Grade 2, because the ulcer involves the bone and requires immediate surgical debridement of all infected tissue.
options.B: Grade 3, as any ulcer with bone involvement and signs of infection like purulent drainage automatically qualifies for this grade.
options.C: Grade 4, since the description mentions 'extending to the bone,' which indicates osteomyelitis and localized gangrene.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with worsening postprandial nausea, early satiety, and intermittent vomiting over the past 6 months. A gastric emptying study confirms the diagnosis of diabetic gastroparesis. Her current diabetes regimen includes metformin 1000mg twice daily and liraglutide 1.8mg daily. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial pharmacological adjustment to manage her gastroparesis?

options.A: Continue the GLP-1 agonist but add metoclopramide 10mg before meals to accelerate gastric emptying while maintaining glycemic control.
options.B: Switch from liraglutide to a sulfonylurea like glimepiride, as GLP-1 agonists are contraindicated and sulfonylureas do not affect gastrointestinal motility.
options.C: Increase the liraglutide dose to 2.4mg daily to improve glycemic control, as better glucose management may resolve the gastroparesis symptoms.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with severe, symptomatic gastroparesis confirmed by gastric emptying study. Her current diabetes regimen includes a basal-bolus insulin regimen. Her HbA1c is 8.5%. Which of the following would be an appropriate next step in her diabetes management?

options.A: Add a GLP-1 receptor agonist to improve glycemic control and promote weight loss, as her gastroparesis symptoms are likely unrelated to her diabetes medications.
options.B: Switch her basal insulin to a premixed insulin formulation to simplify her regimen, as gastroparesis makes meal timing unpredictable.
options.C: Discontinue all insulin and initiate an SGLT2 inhibitor to reduce hyperglycemia without the risk of hypoglycemia from delayed gastric emptying.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a Dexcom G7 CGM reports that her sensor readings have been consistently reading 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick glucose checks for the past 24 hours. She mentions she started taking 1000 mg of vitamin C daily three days ago for immune support. Which of the following is the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to discontinue her CGM immediately as vitamin C permanently damages the sensor's electrochemical components, requiring replacement.
options.B: Tell her to double her vitamin C dose to 2000 mg daily, as higher antioxidant levels will eventually stabilize the sensor readings through improved glucose oxidation.
options.C: Recommend she calibrate her CGM using fingerstick values every hour for the next 48 hours to 'train' the sensor to overcome the vitamin C interference.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a warm, erythematous, and swollen left foot. He reports no history of trauma and minimal pain. His foot pulses are bounding. A provisional diagnosis of acute Charcot neuroarthropathy is considered. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach?

options.A: Immediately start a course of broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics for suspected osteomyelitis, as the warmth and erythema are pathognomonic for infection.
options.B: Perform urgent surgical debridement and biopsy of the swollen area to obtain a definitive tissue diagnosis before initiating any treatment.
options.C: Apply a compression bandage to reduce the swelling and advise the patient to continue weight-bearing to maintain joint mobility and prevent stiffness.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes and peripheral neuropathy presents with a warm, erythematous, and swollen left foot. He reports no recent trauma or fever. X-ray shows midfoot fragmentation and dislocation. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management step for suspected acute Charcot neuroarthropathy?

options.A: Immediately start a course of intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed osteomyelitis, as the warm, red foot indicates infection.
options.B: Prescribe a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) like ibuprofen for pain and inflammation, and advise the patient to continue weight-bearing as tolerated.
options.C: Perform an urgent incision and drainage of the swollen area to relieve pressure and obtain a tissue culture.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a real-time continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick glucose checks for the past two days. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) for immune support three days ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate clinical interpretation and management recommendation?

options.A: The vitamin C is causing hypoglycemia, and the CGM is correctly detecting this trend while the fingerstick is lagging; she should increase her basal insulin dose immediately.
options.B: High-dose vitamin C supplements are known to cause permanent sensor damage; she should stop the CGM immediately and switch to fingerstick monitoring only until she completes her supplement course.
options.C: This is a normal calibration issue; she should manually calibrate her CGM using the elevated fingerstick values to correct the sensor algorithm.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on her shins, consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum (NLD). She is concerned about the appearance and occasional ulceration. Which of the following represents an appropriate management approach for this condition?

options.A: Initiate high-dose systemic corticosteroids as first-line therapy to suppress the inflammatory process and prevent progression.
options.B: Recommend complete bed rest and elevation of legs to reduce blood flow to the lesions, as increased circulation worsens the condition.
options.C: Advise that strict glycemic control with an A1C below 6.0% will reliably lead to complete resolution of existing lesions within 3-6 months.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her fasting glucose is 110 mg/dL and 2-hour postprandial values are consistently 150-160 mg/dL. She is concerned about medication safety. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial pharmacological management?

options.A: Start metformin immediately, as it is completely safe throughout all trimesters and is the only oral agent approved by the FDA for use in pregnancy.
options.B: Start glyburide, as it does not cross the placenta and has a superior safety profile compared to insulin for fetal outcomes.
options.C: Delay pharmacological treatment until the third trimester to minimize any potential fetal drug exposure, focusing only on stricter dietary control until then.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old patient at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her fasting glucose is 110 mg/dL and 1-hour postprandial values are consistently 150-160 mg/dL. She expresses concern about medication safety. Which of the following represents appropriate initial pharmacologic management?

options.A: Start metformin immediately, as it is the safest oral agent in pregnancy and should be used before considering any insulin therapy.
options.B: Initiate glyburide, as it does not cross the placenta and is therefore safer than insulin for both mother and fetus.
options.C: Delay medication initiation until the third trimester to minimize fetal exposure, focusing instead on stricter dietary restriction regardless of glucose levels.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents with a fever and body aches. She asks if she can safely take acetaminophen for symptom relief while wearing her CGM. Which of the following statements about acetaminophen and CGM accuracy is correct?

options.A: Acetaminophen causes falsely low CGM readings, so patients should add 20% to their displayed glucose values when taking this medication.
options.B: All modern CGM systems are completely unaffected by acetaminophen, so she can take any dose without concern for glucose monitoring accuracy.
options.C: Acetaminophen interference only occurs with fingerstick glucose meters, not with CGM systems, so she should rely solely on her CGM readings while taking this medication.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents with a fever and body aches. She reports taking acetaminophen 1000mg every 6 hours for the past 24 hours. Her CGM readings show persistent values in the 180-220 mg/dL range, but her fingerstick glucometer readings are consistently 90-110 mg/dL. When counseling her about this discrepancy, which of the following statements would be appropriate?

options.A: This is expected with most CGMs; acetaminophen causes a predictable upward bias, so you should subtract 40 mg/dL from all CGM readings while taking it.
options.B: The interference is dose-dependent but only affects readings above 200 mg/dL, so you can trust CGM values when they show hypoglycemia during acetaminophen use.
options.C: This confirms your CGM sensor has failed; remove it immediately and insert a new one, as acetaminophen doesn't interfere with modern glucose oxidase-based sensors.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several large, well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on her anterior shins, consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum (NLD). She is concerned about the appearance and occasional ulceration. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial management approach?

options.A: Prescribe high-potency topical corticosteroids with occlusion and advise her that this will completely resolve the lesions and prevent recurrence if applied consistently for 2 weeks.
options.B: Recommend immediate surgical excision of all plaques to prevent malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma, which is a common complication of long-standing NLD.
options.C: Initiate systemic immunosuppression with oral prednisone at 1 mg/kg/day, as this is the first-line treatment to halt disease progression and is required for all patients with NLD.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old pregnant patient at 28 weeks gestation has been diagnosed with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM). She is using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) and asks what her time in range (TIR) targets should be for optimal pregnancy outcomes. Which of the following represents the correct TIR targets for GDM management?

options.A: The primary target should be >70% time in range of 70-140 mg/dL, with less than 4% time below 70 mg/dL, as this matches the general adult diabetes target.
options.B: Aim for >80% time in range of 90-120 mg/dL, with zero tolerance for any readings above 120 mg/dL to prevent fetal macrosomia.
options.C: Target >90% time in range of 60-100 mg/dL, as pregnancy requires tighter control than non-pregnant states, and hypoglycemia is not a concern in GDM.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM). She asks what her daily glucose time-in-range (TIR) target should be. Which of the following is the recommended TIR target for her condition?

options.A: The target is >90% time in range of 70-140 mg/dL, as this matches the tighter non-pregnant adult type 1 diabetes target to prevent fetal macrosomia.
options.B: The target is >70% time in range of 70-180 mg/dL, as this is the standard target for non-pregnant adults with diabetes and is safe for pregnancy.
options.C: The target is >85% time in range of 65-120 mg/dL, as fasting and postprandial thresholds must be lower than in type 2 diabetes to prevent placental vascular complications.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents to the podiatry clinic with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot. On examination, there is a deep ulcer extending to the bone with surrounding erythema and purulent drainage. There is no gangrene or systemic signs of infection. According to the Wagner classification system for diabetic foot ulcers, what grade would this ulcer be classified as?

options.A: Wagner Grade 2, because the ulcer extends to tendon or capsule but not bone, and the presence of infection automatically elevates it from Grade 1.
options.B: Wagner Grade 3, as any ulcer with bone involvement is automatically Grade 3, regardless of the presence or absence of osteomyelitis.
options.C: Wagner Grade 4, because the description of 'extending to bone' with purulent drainage indicates localized gangrene of the forefoot, which defines this grade.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of his right foot. On examination, there is a deep ulcer extending to the bone, with surrounding erythema and purulent drainage. There is no gangrene. According to the Wagner classification system, what grade is this ulcer?

options.A: Wagner Grade 2, because the ulcer extends to the bone and there is evidence of infection, which defines this grade.
options.B: Wagner Grade 3, as any ulcer with osteomyelitis or deep abscess automatically qualifies for this grade, regardless of other features.
options.C: Wagner Grade 1, since the primary feature is a deep ulcer, and the presence of infection does not change the grade if there is no gangrene.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents with worsening nausea, early satiety, and intermittent vomiting over 6 months. She has documented gastroparesis confirmed by gastric emptying scintigraphy. Her current diabetes regimen includes metformin 1000mg twice daily and glargine insulin 30 units at bedtime. Her HbA1c is 8.2%. Which of the following would be an appropriate next step in her diabetes management?

options.A: Add liraglutide 1.8mg daily to improve glycemic control and promote weight loss, as GLP-1 receptor agonists are effective for both diabetes and gastroparesis symptoms.
options.B: Discontinue metformin and switch to a sulfonylurea like glipizide, as metformin is contraindicated in gastroparesis due to its gastrointestinal side effects.
options.C: Continue current therapy and add domperidone 10mg four times daily, as this prokinetic agent has no cardiac contraindications and is first-line for diabetic gastroparesis.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes and severe gastroparesis presents for management. Her current regimen includes insulin glargine and insulin lispro. She has persistent nausea, early satiety, and unpredictable postprandial glucose excursions. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in her pharmacologic management?

options.A: Initiate a GLP-1 receptor agonist to improve glycemic control by slowing gastric emptying and reducing postprandial glucose spikes.
options.B: Add metformin to her insulin regimen to improve insulin sensitivity and help manage her unpredictable glucose levels.
options.C: Prescribe a DPP-4 inhibitor, as these agents are safe and effective for gastroparesis by not affecting gastrointestinal motility.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing glucose values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick readings for the past 2 days. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) for immune support 3 days ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to stop her vitamin C supplements immediately, as they are known to cause falsely low CGM readings that could mask hypoglycemia.
options.B: Tell her this is expected with vitamin C supplementation and she should simply add 50 mg/dL to all her CGM readings to correct for this predictable interference.
options.C: Recommend she switch to taking her vitamin C supplements only at bedtime, as interference is most pronounced when taken with meals and will be negligible overnight.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes and peripheral neuropathy presents with a warm, swollen, erythematous left foot. He reports no recent trauma or foot injury. X-rays show fragmentation and dislocation of the tarsometatarsal joints. What is the most appropriate initial management step?

options.A: Immediately start a course of broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics for suspected osteomyelitis, as the warmth and erythema indicate infection.
options.B: Apply a compression bandage to reduce the swelling and advise the patient to continue weight-bearing to maintain joint mobility.
options.C: Perform an urgent surgical debridement of the affected joints to remove fragmented bone and prevent further destruction.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes and peripheral neuropathy presents with a warm, swollen, erythematous left foot. He reports no recent trauma or foot injury. X-ray shows midfoot fragmentation and joint subluxation. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management step for suspected acute Charcot neuroarthropathy?

options.A: Immediately start a 10-day course of broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics for presumed osteomyelitis, as the warm, swollen foot with erythema indicates infection.
options.B: Prescribe a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) like ibuprofen for pain and inflammation, and advise the patient to continue weight-bearing activities to maintain joint mobility.
options.C: Apply a compression bandage to reduce the swelling and refer to a vascular surgeon for urgent assessment of possible deep vein thrombosis.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a real-time continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing glucose values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick meter checks for the past two days. She mentions she started taking a high-dose vitamin C supplement (1000 mg twice daily) for immune support three days ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to stop the vitamin C supplement immediately, as it causes irreversible sensor damage requiring sensor replacement.
options.B: Tell her to double-check all her CGM readings with fingersticks and add 50 mg/dL to any CGM reading to account for the vitamin C interference.
options.C: Explain that vitamin C only affects older CGM models and recommend she switch to a different brand of CGM that is completely immune to this interference.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her obstetrician is considering pharmacotherapy. Regarding the use of metformin in this context, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: Metformin is contraindicated throughout pregnancy as it is a known teratogen and should be stopped immediately upon pregnancy confirmation.
options.B: Metformin is the first-line pharmacologic agent for GDM and should be initiated immediately, as insulin is only reserved for cases of severe hyperglycemia with ketosis.
options.C: Metformin is safe to use in the second and third trimesters but must be discontinued by 36 weeks gestation to prevent neonatal lactic acidosis during delivery.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old woman with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes at 18 weeks gestation has failed to achieve glycemic targets with diet and exercise alone. Her fasting glucose is consistently 105-115 mg/dL, and her 1-hour postprandial values are 150-165 mg/dL. She is concerned about medication safety. Which of the following statements regarding metformin use in her pregnancy is correct?

options.A: Metformin should be avoided entirely as it crosses the placenta and is classified as FDA Pregnancy Category X, posing a high risk of major fetal malformations.
options.B: Metformin is safe but should only be used in the third trimester, as first and second trimester use is associated with an increased risk of preterm labor and preeclampsia.
options.C: Metformin is the preferred first-line agent over insulin because it does not cross the placenta and has been proven to reduce the risk of neonatal hypoglycemia and macrosomia more effectively than insulin.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents with a fever and body aches. She asks if she can safely take acetaminophen for symptom relief while wearing her CGM. Which of the following statements about acetaminophen and CGM accuracy is correct?

options.A: Acetaminophen causes a predictable, linear overestimation of glucose readings, so you should add 20 mg/dL to all CGM values while taking it.
options.B: Only high-dose acetaminophen (over 4 grams daily) interferes with CGM readings, so standard 650 mg doses every 6 hours are completely safe.
options.C: All modern CGMs are now completely immune to acetaminophen interference due to updated sensor chemistry, so no precautions are needed.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a Dexcom G6 CGM reports that her sensor readings have been showing values around 250 mg/dL for several hours, but she feels fine and her fingerstick glucose meter shows 110 mg/dL. She mentions she took acetaminophen 1000 mg for a headache 3 hours ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate clinical interpretation and action regarding this CGM discrepancy?

options.A: The acetaminophen is causing a falsely low CGM reading, so the actual glucose is likely around 250 mg/dL and she should administer a correction insulin dose based on the CGM value.
options.B: This is a known interaction where acetaminophen causes CGM readings to be artificially elevated; she should calibrate the sensor using her fingerstick value to correct the reading.
options.C: Acetaminophen interference only affects older CGM models; since she's using a Dexcom G6, this cannot be the cause and she should replace the sensor immediately as it has failed.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on her anterior shins, consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum. She is concerned about the appearance and occasional ulceration. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial management approach?

options.A: Prescribe high-potency topical corticosteroids with occlusion and advise that this will completely resolve the lesions if glycemic control is tightened.
options.B: Recommend immediate surgical excision of all plaques to prevent malignant transformation, as necrobiosis lipoidica is a known precursor to squamous cell carcinoma.
options.C: Initiate systemic immunosuppression with oral prednisone at 1 mg/kg/day, as this is the first-line treatment to halt disease progression and promote healing.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is reviewing her continuous glucose monitor (CGM) data with you. She has been monitoring diligently and asks what her primary time-in-range (TIR) target should be for the remainder of her pregnancy. Based on current international consensus guidelines for GDM, what is the recommended primary TIR target?

options.A: The target should be the same as for non-pregnant adults with type 2 diabetes: >70% of readings between 70-180 mg/dL (3.9-10.0 mmol/L).
options.B: Given the risks of fetal macrosomia, the target should be very strict: >90% of readings between 70-110 mg/dL (3.9-6.1 mmol/L).
options.C: To prevent maternal hypoglycemia, which is the greatest concern in GDM, the target should be liberal: >50% of readings between 90-200 mg/dL (5.0-11.1 mmol/L).
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old pregnant patient at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM). During a routine prenatal visit, you review her ambulatory glucose profile. According to current international consensus guidelines, what is the recommended primary target for time in range (TIR) for pregnant individuals with GDM?

options.A: The target should be >90% time in range (70-140 mg/dL), as this is the same stringent target used for non-pregnant adults with type 1 diabetes to prevent long-term complications.
options.B: The target should be >85% time in range (63-140 mg/dL), using the lower threshold of 63 mg/dL to aggressively prevent any maternal hypoglycemia, which is the main concern in GDM.
options.C: The target should be >70% time in range (70-180 mg/dL), as this is the standard target for non-pregnant adults with diabetes and is sufficient to prevent fetal macrosomia.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents to the clinic with a foot ulcer. On examination, there is a full-thickness skin defect with exposed tendon on the plantar surface of her right foot, but no bone is visible. There is surrounding erythema and mild edema, but no signs of systemic infection. According to the Wagner classification system, what is the grade of this ulcer?

options.A: Grade 1, because the ulcer is superficial and involves only the skin, without deeper tissue involvement.
options.B: Grade 3, because the presence of exposed tendon indicates deep tissue involvement with osteomyelitis requiring bone biopsy.
options.C: Grade 4, as any ulcer with exposed tendon automatically indicates localized gangrene requiring partial foot amputation.
options.D: None of the above
