options.B: Tell her this is expected and harmless; vitamin C is an antioxidant that improves CGM accuracy by stabilizing glucose oxidase, so she should trust the higher CGM values over fingersticks.
options.C: Recommend she double her vitamin C dose to 2000 mg twice daily, as this will correct the sensor's calibration and bring readings back into alignment with fingerstick values.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents with a fever and body aches. She asks if she can safely take acetaminophen for symptom relief while wearing her CGM. Which of the following statements about acetaminophen interference with CGM readings is correct?

options.A: Acetaminophen causes falsely elevated CGM readings in all sensor models, so she should double-check her fingerstick glucose and add 50 mg/dL to account for the interference.
options.B: Only high-dose intravenous acetaminophen causes interference; standard oral doses under 1000 mg are completely safe and won't affect any CGM accuracy.
options.C: She should temporarily remove her CGM sensor when taking acetaminophen and reapply it 24 hours after her last dose to avoid any measurement artifacts.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents with worsening nausea, early satiety, and intermittent vomiting over 6 months. She reports significant postprandial bloating. A gastric emptying study confirms severe gastroparesis. Her current medications include metformin and a GLP-1 receptor agonist. What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological adjustment to manage her gastroparesis?

options.A: Increase the dose of her GLP-1 receptor agonist, as this class improves gastric motility and will help resolve her symptoms.
options.B: Switch from her current GLP-1 receptor agonist to a DPP-4 inhibitor, as these have similar glycemic benefits without affecting gastric emptying.
options.C: Add a second GLP-1 receptor agonist at a lower dose to provide a more sustained effect on gastric emptying while maintaining glycemic control.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents to the podiatry clinic with a chronic, non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot. On examination, the ulcer is 2 cm in diameter, extends to the bone, and there is surrounding erythema and warmth. There is no crepitus or foul-smelling discharge. Which of the following correctly applies the Wagner classification to this presentation?

options.A: This is Wagner Grade 2 because the ulcer involves the bone, and the erythema indicates a deep infection requiring intravenous antibiotics.
options.B: This is Wagner Grade 3 because the ulcer extends to the bone, and the presence of osteomyelitis automatically upgrades any ulcer to at least Grade 3.
options.C: This is Wagner Grade 1 because the ulcer is superficial and less than 3 cm in diameter; the erythema is a sign of cellulitis, not a deeper complication.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old patient at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has a fasting glucose of 110 mg/dL and postprandial values consistently above 140 mg/dL despite dietary modification. Her BMI is 32 kg/m². She asks about medication options beyond insulin. Which of the following statements about metformin use in her pregnancy is correct?

options.A: Metformin is absolutely contraindicated throughout all trimesters of pregnancy due to proven teratogenic effects on fetal organ development.
options.B: Metformin should be initiated immediately as first-line therapy, as it crosses the placenta and provides direct glycemic benefits to the fetus, reducing the need for maternal insulin.
options.C: Metformin is safe to use but only during the third trimester, as early exposure increases the risk of childhood obesity and metabolic syndrome in the offspring.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and severe gastroparesis confirmed by gastric emptying study presents for diabetes management review. Her current HbA1c is 8.5%. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in her pharmacologic management?

options.A: Initiate a GLP-1 receptor agonist to improve glycemic control and promote weight loss, as gastroparesis is not a contraindication.
options.B: Start metformin extended-release to reduce gastrointestinal side effects and improve insulin sensitivity.
options.C: Add acarbose to slow carbohydrate absorption and reduce postprandial glucose spikes.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is reviewing her continuous glucose monitor (CGM) data. Her obstetrician asks you, the diabetes educator, what the primary time-in-range (TIR) target should be for her pregnancy. Which of the following represents the correct TIR target for GDM?

options.A: The target should be the same as for non-pregnant adults with type 2 diabetes: >70% of readings between 70-180 mg/dL.
options.B: Given the risks of fetal macrosomia, the target should be stricter than standard pregnancy targets, aiming for >90% of readings between 65-110 mg/dL.
options.C: Since she is diet-controlled, less stringent targets are acceptable; aiming for >50% of readings between 70-140 mg/dL is sufficient to prevent complications.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 20 years presents with several well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on both shins, diagnosed as necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum (NLD). She is concerned about the appearance and occasional ulceration. Which of the following represents an appropriate management approach?

options.A: Initiate high-dose oral corticosteroids as first-line therapy to suppress the inflammatory process and prevent progression.
options.B: Recommend complete bed rest and elevation of legs to reduce blood flow to the lesions, as increased circulation worsens the condition.
options.C: Apply high-potency topical steroids with occlusion daily until lesions resolve, as this is the most effective method to clear plaques permanently.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on both shins, consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum. She is concerned about the appearance and occasional ulceration. What is the most appropriate initial management approach?

options.A: Prescribe high-potency topical corticosteroids with occlusion and advise her that this will completely resolve the lesions if she achieves tighter glycemic control with an HbA1c below 6.0%.
options.B: Recommend immediate wide surgical excision of all lesions to prevent malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma, which is a common complication of long-standing necrobiosis lipoidica.
options.C: Start systemic immunosuppression with oral prednisone at 40mg daily for 4 weeks, as this is the first-line treatment for active necrobiosis lipoidica lesions regardless of size or symptoms.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old patient at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes (GDM) is reviewing her continuous glucose monitor (CGM) data. She asks what her daily time-in-range (TIR) target should be. Based on current international consensus guidelines for GDM, which of the following represents the appropriate TIR target?

options.A: The target is >90% time in range (70-140 mg/dL), as this is the same stringent target used for pregnant patients with pre-existing type 1 diabetes to prevent macrosomia.
options.B: The target is >70% time in range (63-140 mg/dL), using a lower threshold to account for the increased risk of nocturnal hypoglycemia in pregnancy.
options.C: There is no established TIR target for GDM; management should rely solely on fasting and postprandial fingerstick values, as CGM is not validated in pregnancy.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes and peripheral neuropathy presents with a warm, swollen, erythematous left foot. He reports no recent trauma or fever. X-ray shows midfoot fragmentation and dislocation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Immediately start intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics for suspected osteomyelitis, as the erythema and warmth indicate infection.
options.B: Apply a compression bandage to reduce the swelling and advise the patient to continue weight-bearing to maintain joint mobility.
options.C: Perform an urgent incision and drainage of the swollen area to relieve pressure and obtain cultures.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a warm, swollen, erythematous left foot. He reports no recent trauma and minimal pain despite the significant swelling. His temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), and his white blood cell count is normal. Based on the suspicion of acute Charcot neuropathic osteoarthropathy, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach?

options.A: Immediately start broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics for presumed osteomyelitis, as the erythema and swelling are pathognomonic for infection in a diabetic foot.
options.B: Perform urgent surgical debridement and obtain deep bone cultures to confirm the diagnosis before initiating any treatment.
options.C: Apply a compressive bandage to reduce the swelling and encourage immediate weight-bearing to prevent joint stiffness.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a Dexcom G7 continuous glucose monitor (CGM) calls her diabetes educator. She has been taking acetaminophen 1000 mg every 6 hours for a tension headache over the past 24 hours and is concerned about potential CGM interference. Which of the following statements about acetaminophen interference with her CGM is correct?

options.A: Acetaminophen causes falsely elevated glucose readings on all current-generation CGMs, so she should add 20% to her insulin doses while taking it.
options.B: The interference is dose-dependent, so she should switch to fingerstick glucose monitoring only when taking more than 500 mg of acetaminophen at once.
options.C: Acetaminophen interference is a known issue with older CGM models but has been completely eliminated in the Dexcom G7, so she can trust her readings without concern.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports persistently elevated sensor readings despite feeling well. She mentions she recently started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (2,000 mg daily) for immune support. Which of the following represents the most appropriate clinical guidance regarding this situation?

options.A: Advise her that vitamin C has no effect on CGM readings, and the discrepancy must be due to sensor failure requiring immediate replacement.
options.B: Tell her to double-check all high CGM readings with a fingerstick, but continue the vitamin C as it provides important antioxidant benefits for diabetes management.
options.C: Recommend she switch to taking her vitamin C supplement only at bedtime to minimize interference, as this timing avoids most daytime glucose monitoring needs.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents to the podiatry clinic. She has a chronic, non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her left foot. On examination, the ulcer measures 2 cm in diameter, extends through the subcutaneous tissue to expose the underlying tendon, but there is no bone involvement. There is no clinical evidence of infection. According to the Wagner classification system for diabetic foot ulcers, what is the grade of this ulcer?

options.A: Grade 1, because the ulcer is superficial and involves only the epidermis and dermis.
options.B: Grade 3, because tendon exposure automatically indicates osteomyelitis requiring bone debridement.
options.C: Grade 2, as the ulcer extends to tendon but without infection, which is the defining feature of this grade.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her fasting glucose is 110 mg/dL and 1-hour postprandial values are consistently 150-160 mg/dL. She is concerned about medication safety. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial pharmacological management approach?

options.A: Initiate metformin immediately, as it is the safest oral agent and should be used first-line in all pregnant patients with GDM, even before considering insulin.
options.B: Start glyburide, as it does not cross the placenta and has superior safety data compared to insulin for fetal outcomes in the first trimester.
options.C: Delay pharmacological treatment until the third trimester to minimize any potential fetal exposure, focusing only on stricter dietary control regardless of glucose levels.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing glucose values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick readings for the past 24 hours. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) for immune support two days ago. What is the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to stop the CGM immediately and switch to fingerstick monitoring only, as vitamin C permanently damages the sensor's electrochemical detection system.
options.B: Tell her to double her vitamin C dose to overcome the interference, as higher antioxidant levels will eventually stabilize the sensor readings.
options.C: Recommend she calibrate the CGM more frequently using fingerstick values while continuing vitamin C, as this will correct the systematic error caused by the supplement.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a Dexcom G6 CGM reports to her endocrinologist that her sensor readings have been showing values around 250 mg/dL for several hours, but she feels fine and her fingerstick meter reads 110 mg/dL. She mentions she took acetaminophen 1000 mg for a headache 4 hours ago. What is the most appropriate clinical interpretation and action regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: The acetaminophen is causing the CGM to read falsely low, so the patient should increase her insulin dose based on the CGM reading of 250 mg/dL to correct for the actual hyperglycemia.
options.B: Acetaminophen interference is only significant with older CGM models; the Dexcom G6 is completely immune, so the discrepancy must be due to sensor failure requiring immediate replacement.
options.C: The patient should continue to trust the CGM reading over fingerstick values whenever acetaminophen is involved, as the medication alters capillary blood glucose measurements more than interstitial fluid readings.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents with worsening nausea, early satiety, and intermittent vomiting over 6 months. A gastric emptying study confirms severe gastroparesis. Her current medications include metformin 1000mg twice daily and glimepiride 4mg daily. Her HbA1c is 8.2%. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in her glycemic management?

options.A: Start a GLP-1 receptor agonist to improve glycemic control while simultaneously addressing her weight, as these agents slow gastric emptying which may help her symptoms.
options.B: Discontinue metformin and initiate a DPP-4 inhibitor, as metformin is known to cause gastroparesis and should be avoided in this condition.
options.C: Continue current oral agents and add a short-acting insulin secretagogue before each meal to better match her unpredictable gastric emptying.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents to the podiatry clinic with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot. On examination, there is a deep ulcer that probes to bone, with surrounding erythema and purulent drainage. There is no exposed bone or tendon visible. According to the Wagner classification system, what is the grade of this ulcer?

options.A: This is a Wagner Grade 2 ulcer because there is a deep ulcer with bone involvement, as evidenced by the positive probe-to-bone test.
options.B: This is a Wagner Grade 3 ulcer because there is purulent drainage and surrounding erythema, indicating a deep infection.
options.C: This is a Wagner Grade 1 ulcer because the ulcer is deep but there is no exposed bone, tendon, or joint capsule.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old woman with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes at 16 weeks gestation has failed to achieve glycemic targets with diet and exercise alone. Her fasting glucose is consistently 105-115 mg/dL, and her 1-hour postprandial values are 150-165 mg/dL. She is concerned about medication safety. Which of the following statements about initiating metformin therapy in this context is correct?

options.A: Metformin should be avoided entirely in pregnancy because it crosses the placenta and causes fetal hypoglycemia and lactic acidosis.
options.B: Metformin is the first-line pharmacological agent for GDM and should be started immediately at a high dose of 2000 mg daily to achieve rapid glycemic control.
options.C: Metformin is safe in pregnancy but only for women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS); for isolated GDM, insulin must be used instead.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and severe gastroparesis presents for medication review. Her current regimen includes metformin 1000mg twice daily. Her HbA1c is 8.2% despite maximal metformin dosing. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in her glycemic management?

options.A: Initiate a GLP-1 receptor agonist to improve glycemic control while simultaneously addressing her gastroparesis symptoms through enhanced gastric motility.
options.B: Add a DPP-4 inhibitor, as these agents are specifically recommended for patients with gastroparesis due to their neutral effects on gastric emptying.
options.C: Switch from metformin to a sulfonylurea, as metformin is contraindicated in gastroparesis due to its gastrointestinal side effects.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old pregnant patient at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) for self-management. During a telehealth visit, she asks what her primary glucose time-in-range (TIR) target should be for the remainder of her pregnancy. Which of the following represents the correct TIR target for GDM?

options.A: The target should be the same as for non-pregnant adults with type 2 diabetes: >70% of readings between 70-180 mg/dL.
options.B: Given the risks of fetal macrosomia, the target should be very strict: >90% of readings between 65-110 mg/dL.
options.C: Since she is diet-controlled, the target can be more lenient: >50% of readings between 70-140 mg/dL is sufficient.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 20 years presents with several large, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on her shins, consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum. She is concerned about the appearance and asks about management. Which of the following represents an appropriate initial management approach?

options.A: Immediately start high-potency topical corticosteroids under occlusion and continue indefinitely to prevent progression.
options.B: Recommend surgical excision of all lesions to prevent malignant transformation, as this condition has a high risk of developing into squamous cell carcinoma.
options.C: Prescribe a course of oral tetracycline antibiotics, as this is primarily a bacterial infection of the dermis requiring systemic antimicrobial therapy.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on both shins, diagnosed as necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum. She is concerned about the appearance and asks about management. Which of the following represents appropriate initial management?

options.A: Start high-potency topical corticosteroids with occlusion and advise that this will completely resolve the lesions if blood glucose control is tightened.
options.B: Perform an immediate biopsy of all lesions to rule out malignancy, as necrobiosis lipoidica has a high malignant transformation rate to squamous cell carcinoma.
options.C: Prescribe systemic immunosuppressants like methotrexate as first-line therapy, since this is an autoimmune condition directly linked to her type 1 diabetes.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks gestation was recently diagnosed with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) and started on continuous glucose monitoring (CGM). During her follow-up visit, she asks what her daily glucose time-in-range (TIR) target should be. Based on current international consensus guidelines for GDM, what is the recommended TIR target?

options.A: The primary target should be >90% time in range, defined as 70-140 mg/dL (3.9-7.8 mmol/L), with less than 1% time below range.
options.B: For GDM, the target should be more stringent than for type 1 diabetes in pregnancy, aiming for >95% time between 65-120 mg/dL (3.6-6.7 mmol/L) to prevent macrosomia.
options.C: Since she is using CGM, she should aim for >85% time between 80-160 mg/dL (4.4-8.9 mmol/L), as this wider range accounts for normal pregnancy glucose variability.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a warm, erythematous, and swollen left foot. He reports no history of trauma and denies pain. On examination, the foot is significantly edematous with bounding pulses and intact sensation. An X-ray shows bony fragmentation and joint subluxation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Immediately start intravenous antibiotics for presumed cellulitis or osteomyelitis, as the warmth and erythema indicate infection.
options.B: Apply a compression bandage to reduce the swelling and advise the patient to continue weight-bearing to maintain joint mobility.
options.C: Schedule an outpatient orthopedic consultation in 2-3 weeks, as this is a chronic, non-urgent condition that does not require immediate intervention.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents to the clinic with a warm, swollen, and erythematous left foot. He reports no history of trauma and minimal pain despite the significant swelling. His foot pulses are bounding, and he has profound peripheral neuropathy. You suspect acute Charcot neuroarthropathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?

options.A: Immediately start a 10-day course of broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics, as the erythema and warmth are pathognomonic for osteomyelitis requiring urgent treatment.
options.B: Apply a compression bandage to reduce the swelling and advise the patient to continue weight-bearing as tolerated to maintain joint mobility.
options.C: Order an urgent bone scan; if it is positive, it confirms the diagnosis of Charcot foot and rules out infection.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents with a fever and body aches. She reports taking acetaminophen 1000mg every 6 hours for the past 24 hours. Her CGM readings have been showing values around 180-220 mg/dL, but her fingerstick glucose checks show values of 110-130 mg/dL. She asks about this discrepancy. Which of the following is the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this situation?

options.A: The acetaminophen is causing hypoglycemia, so the CGM is reading falsely low; she should increase her insulin doses based on the CGM readings to correct this.
options.B: This is a known interaction where acetaminophen causes CGM sensors to read falsely low; she should rely on fingerstick values and consider the CGM temporarily unreliable.
options.C: The discrepancy indicates sensor failure; she should immediately remove the current sensor and calibrate a new one with her current fingerstick value to restore accuracy.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a real-time continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing glucose values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick readings for the past 24 hours. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) three days ago for immune support. What is the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to continue taking the vitamin C supplements but calibrate her CGM more frequently (every 2-3 hours) to correct for the interference, as this will normalize the readings.
options.B: Tell her that vitamin C has no effect on CGM accuracy, and the discrepancy is likely due to sensor failure; she should replace the sensor immediately and continue the supplements.
options.C: Recommend she switch to taking her vitamin C supplements only at bedtime, as this timing will avoid interference with daytime glucose readings while maintaining the supplement's benefits.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old pregnant patient at 28 weeks gestation is diagnosed with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM). She is using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) and asks what her daily time in range (TIR) target should be for optimal pregnancy outcomes. Based on current international consensus guidelines for GDM, what is the recommended TIR target?

options.A: The target is >90% time in range, defined as glucose between 3.9-7.8 mmol/L (70-140 mg/dL), with less than 1% time below range.
options.B: For GDM, the primary focus should be on fasting glucose alone; time in range targets are not validated in pregnancy and should not be used.
options.C: The target is >85% time in range, but the range should be wider at 4.0-10.0 mmol/L (72-180 mg/dL) to account for normal pregnancy insulin resistance.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents to urgent care with fever and body aches. She reports taking acetaminophen 1000mg every 6 hours for the past 24 hours. Her CGM readings show persistent values of 180-220 mg/dL, but her fingerstick glucometer readings are consistently 90-110 mg/dL. When counseling her about this discrepancy, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: The acetaminophen is causing a true hyperglycemic effect, so she should increase her basal insulin by 20% while taking it.
options.B: This is a known CGM sensor failure requiring immediate sensor replacement; acetaminophen does not affect modern CGM technology.
options.C: The acetaminophen is interfering with the CGM's glucose oxidase enzyme, causing falsely low readings that should be disregarded.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a warm, erythematous, and swollen left foot. He reports no history of trauma and minimal pain despite the significant swelling. His foot pulses are bounding, and he has profound peripheral neuropathy. You suspect acute Charcot neuroarthropathy. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?

options.A: Immediately start a course of broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics for suspected osteomyelitis, as the warmth and redness indicate infection.
options.B: Perform urgent surgical debridement and drainage of the swollen area to relieve pressure and obtain tissue for culture.
options.C: Apply a compression bandage to reduce the edema and advise the patient to continue weight-bearing to maintain joint mobility.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing glucose values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick readings for the past 24 hours. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) three days ago for immune support. Regarding the accuracy of her CGM readings, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: The discrepancy is likely due to vitamin C interfering with the glucose oxidase enzyme in the sensor, causing falsely elevated readings; she should double-check all readings with fingersticks and consider temporarily stopping her CGM.
options.B: Vitamin C causes falsely low CGM readings by competing with glucose for the sensor's electrochemical reaction; she should add 40-60 mg/dL to all CGM values while taking the supplement.
options.C: This is a known calibration error; she should perform a manual calibration of her CGM using her current fingerstick value, which will correct the sensor algorithm for the duration of the sensor wear.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her fasting glucose is 110 mg/dL and 1-hour postprandial values average 160 mg/dL. She is concerned about medication safety. Which of the following represents an appropriate management approach?

options.A: Start metformin immediately as it is the safest first-line pharmacological agent in all trimesters of pregnancy and does not cross the placenta in significant amounts.
options.B: Delay pharmacological treatment until the third trimester to minimize fetal exposure, as insulin should only be initiated if fasting glucose exceeds 140 mg/dL after 28 weeks.
options.C: Prescribe glyburide as the preferred first-line agent because it has superior safety data compared to insulin and does not require dose titration or glucose monitoring.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old woman with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes at 16 weeks gestation has not achieved glycemic targets with diet and exercise alone. Her fasting glucose is consistently 105-115 mg/dL, and postprandial values are 150-165 mg/dL. She is concerned about medication safety. Which of the following statements about initiating metformin in this context is correct?

options.A: Metformin should be avoided entirely in pregnancy as it crosses the placenta and is classified as FDA Pregnancy Category D, posing a high risk of major fetal malformations.
options.B: Metformin is safe to initiate, but the dose must be kept below 500 mg daily to prevent fetal lactic acidosis, and it should be discontinued at 36 weeks to avoid neonatal hypoglycemia.
options.C: Metformin is the preferred first-line agent over insulin because it does not cross the placenta, and its use is associated with lower maternal weight gain and reduced risk of neonatal macrosomia compared to insulin.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a real-time continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing glucose values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick readings for the past 24 hours. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) for immune support two days ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to continue taking the vitamin C supplements as they are beneficial, but to calibrate her CGM sensor twice daily using fingerstick values to correct for this known interference.
options.B: Tell her that vitamin C causes falsely low CGM readings, not high ones, so the discrepancy must be due to sensor failure, and she should replace the sensor immediately.
options.C: Explain that this is a normal physiological effect of vitamin C improving insulin sensitivity, and she should reduce her basal insulin by 20% to match the lower actual glucose levels indicated by fingersticks.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents with worsening nausea, early satiety, and intermittent vomiting over 6 months. She has documented gastroparesis confirmed by gastric emptying study. Her current HbA1c is 8.2%. Her primary care provider is considering adding a GLP-1 receptor agonist to improve her glycemic control. Which of the following statements about managing this patient's diabetes is correct?

options.A: GLP-1 receptor agonists are recommended as first-line therapy for patients with gastroparesis because they slow gastric emptying, which helps control postprandial glucose spikes.
options.B: Starting a GLP-1 receptor agonist is safe if the dose is titrated very slowly and the patient is advised to eat smaller, more frequent meals to manage the gastroparesis symptoms.
options.C: GLP-1 receptor agonists should be avoided, but SGLT2 inhibitors are the preferred alternative as they don't affect gastrointestinal motility and help with weight loss.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several well-demarcated, atrophic, yellowish-brown plaques with telangiectasias on her anterior shins, consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum (NLD). She is concerned about the appearance and occasional ulceration. Which of the following represents an appropriate management approach?

options.A: Initiate high-dose systemic corticosteroids as first-line therapy to suppress the inflammatory process and prevent progression.
options.B: Advise that tight glycemic control with an A1C target of <6.0% is the primary treatment and will reliably lead to lesion resolution.
options.C: Recommend complete surgical excision of all lesions, as this is curative and prevents future malignant transformation risk.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 20 years presents with several large, yellowish-brown, atrophic plaques with telangiectasias and raised violaceous borders on both shins, consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum. She is concerned about the appearance and occasional ulceration. What is the most appropriate initial management approach?

options.A: Prescribe high-potency topical corticosteroids with occlusion and advise her that this will completely resolve the lesions within 2-3 weeks if she maintains excellent glycemic control.
options.B: Recommend immediate surgical excision of all lesions to prevent malignant transformation, as necrobiosis lipoidica has a well-documented risk of progressing to squamous cell carcinoma.
options.C: Initiate systemic immunosuppression with oral prednisone at 1 mg/kg/day, as this is the first-line treatment for necrobiosis lipoidica and will address the underlying granulomatous inflammation.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents to the podiatry clinic with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot. On examination, the ulcer is deep, exposing bone and tendon, but there is no evidence of abscess formation or osteomyelitis. According to the Wagner classification system, what is the grade of this ulcer?

options.A: Grade 2, because the ulcer extends to tendon and bone, which defines full-thickness involvement.
options.B: Grade 3, as any ulcer with exposed bone automatically indicates osteomyelitis requiring this classification.
options.C: Grade 4, since the presence of exposed bone signifies localized gangrene, which is the defining feature of this grade.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents to the clinic with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot. On examination, you note a full-thickness ulcer extending to the subcutaneous tissue, but there is no exposed bone, tendon, or joint capsule. There is surrounding erythema and mild edema, but no signs of systemic infection. According to the Wagner classification system, what is the grade of this ulcer?

options.A: This is a Wagner Grade 2 ulcer because it involves full-thickness skin loss with exposed bone, which requires urgent surgical debridement.
options.B: This is a Wagner Grade 1 ulcer since it's superficial and limited to the skin, and should be managed with topical antibiotics and daily dressing changes alone.
options.C: This is a Wagner Grade 3 ulcer as it shows signs of deep tissue infection with osteomyelitis, warranting immediate IV antibiotics and possible amputation.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a real-time continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents with a fever and body aches. She reports her CGM readings have been showing values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick checks. She mentions she took acetaminophen 1000 mg four hours ago for symptom relief. Regarding the CGM discrepancy, which of the following represents appropriate clinical guidance?

options.A: Advise her that acetaminophen causes falsely low CGM readings with this sensor model, so she should add 50 mg/dL to all CGM values for the next 24 hours.
options.B: Tell her to stop the CGM immediately as acetaminophen interference can cause permanent sensor damage, and she should switch to fingerstick monitoring for the remainder of the sensor's 10-day lifespan.
options.C: Explain that only prescription-strength acetaminophen-opioid combinations cause interference, so she can safely continue taking over-the-counter acetaminophen alone without adjusting her diabetes management.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 54-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a warm, erythematous, and swollen left foot. He reports no history of trauma and denies pain. His HbA1c is 9.2%. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach?

options.A: Immediately start broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics for suspected cellulitis, as the warmth and redness indicate infection requiring urgent treatment.
options.B: Obtain an urgent surgical consultation for possible incision and drainage, as this presentation is consistent with a deep abscess requiring surgical management.
options.C: Prescribe a course of oral NSAIDs and advise rest, ice, compression, and elevation (RICE) for presumed gout or traumatic inflammation.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 8 years presents with worsening symptoms of gastroparesis, including early satiety, nausea, and postprandial bloating. Her current medications include metformin and a GLP-1 receptor agonist. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial management step regarding her diabetes medications?

options.A: Increase the dose of the GLP-1 receptor agonist to improve glycemic control, as gastroparesis symptoms are unrelated to this medication class.
options.B: Switch from the GLP-1 receptor agonist to a DPP-4 inhibitor, but continue both it and metformin since gastroparesis is a chronic complication that doesn't require medication adjustment.
options.C: Discontinue metformin first, as it is the most likely cause of her gastrointestinal symptoms, while continuing the GLP-1 agonist for its cardiovascular benefits.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is starting continuous glucose monitoring (CGM). According to current international consensus guidelines, what is the recommended target for time in range (TIR) for GDM management?

options.A: The primary target should be achieving a TIR of >90% (70-180 mg/dL), as this matches the general diabetes population target to ensure optimal fetal outcomes.
options.B: A TIR of >80% (63-140 mg/dL) is recommended, with postprandial glucose being the most critical metric, so fasting values can be safely ignored if postprandial targets are met.
options.C: Since pregnancy requires tighter control, the target should be >95% TIR with a range of 70-120 mg/dL, and any reading above 120 mg/dL should be considered a treatment failure.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) for self-management. She asks what her primary daily glucose time-in-range (TIR) target should be. Which of the following is the correct target?

options.A: The target is the same as for non-pregnant adults with type 2 diabetes: >70% of readings between 70-180 mg/dL.
options.B: Given the risks of fetal macrosomia, the target should be stricter than standard pregnancy targets: >90% of readings between 65-110 mg/dL.
options.C: Since she is diet-controlled, the target can be more lenient to avoid hypoglycemia: >50% of readings between 70-140 mg/dL is sufficient.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) calls her diabetes educator. She reports that her CGM readings have been showing values around 250 mg/dL for the past 4 hours, but her fingerstick glucose checks consistently show values around 120 mg/dL. She mentions she took acetaminophen 1000 mg for a headache 5 hours ago. What is the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this CGM discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to calibrate her CGM immediately using the 120 mg/dL fingerstick value, as this will correct the sensor's algorithm and override the acetaminophen interference.
options.B: Tell her to disregard all CGM readings for the next 24 hours and rely solely on fingerstick testing, as acetaminophen permanently damages the sensor's chemistry for a full day.
options.C: Recommend she switch to ibuprofen for future pain relief and continue using the current CGM readings, as the acetaminophen effect will dissipate within the next hour.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents to the clinic with a warm, erythematous, and swollen right foot. He reports no history of trauma and denies any pain. His HbA1c is 8.2%. On examination, the foot is significantly edematous with bounding pedal pulses and intact sensation to monofilament testing. A plain X-ray shows bony fragmentation and joint subluxation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Immediately start intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics for suspected osteomyelitis, as the warm, erythematous foot with bony changes on X-ray is diagnostic of infection.
options.B: Prescribe a course of oral NSAIDs for pain and inflammation, and advise the patient to continue weight-bearing activities to maintain joint mobility.
options.C: Schedule an outpatient MRI of the foot in 4-6 weeks to confirm the diagnosis, as the acute phase of Charcot arthropathy is not a medical emergency.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing glucose values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick readings for the past 24 hours. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) three days ago for immune support. What is the most appropriate initial clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to stop the CGM immediately and switch to fingerstick monitoring only, as vitamin C permanently damages the sensor's electrochemical detection system.
options.B: Tell her to double her vitamin C dose to 2000 mg twice daily, as this will help stabilize the CGM readings by saturating the interference effect.
options.C: Recommend she calibrate her CGM more frequently (every 2-3 hours) using fingerstick values to 'train' the sensor to overcome the vitamin C interference.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old woman at 14 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her fasting glucose is 105 mg/dL and 2-hour postprandial values are consistently 150-165 mg/dL. She is concerned about medication safety. Which of the following statements about initiating metformin in this context is correct?

options.A: Metformin should be avoided entirely in pregnancy as it crosses the placenta and is classified as FDA Category X, posing a high risk of major congenital malformations.
options.B: Metformin is the first-line pharmacological agent for GDM, but it must be stopped immediately at 36 weeks gestation to prevent neonatal lactic acidosis during delivery.
options.C: While metformin can be used, insulin is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy due to its potential to cause fetal macrosomia, making metformin the only safe pharmacological option.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her obstetrician is considering pharmacotherapy. Which of the following statements about metformin use in this context is accurate?

options.A: Metformin should be avoided entirely in pregnancy as it crosses the placenta and is classified as FDA Pregnancy Category X, posing unacceptable teratogenic risk.
options.B: Metformin is the first-line pharmacological agent for GDM, as it is proven superior to insulin for both maternal glycemic control and prevention of neonatal macrosomia, with no increased risk of preterm birth.
options.C: While metformin can be used, it must be discontinued by 36 weeks gestation to prevent neonatal lactic acidosis during delivery, and all patients should be switched to insulin at that point.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick glucometer checks for the past two days. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) for immune support three days ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to immediately stop her CGM use and switch to fingerstick monitoring only, as vitamin C permanently damages the sensor's electrochemical detection system.
options.B: Recommend she double her vitamin C dose to 2000 mg twice daily, as this will help stabilize the CGM readings by saturating the interference mechanism.
options.C: Tell her to calibrate her CGM more frequently using her fingerstick values while continuing the vitamin C, as this will correct for the consistent overestimation.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents with worsening nausea, early satiety, and intermittent vomiting over 6 months. She reports significant postprandial bloating and has lost 8 kg unintentionally. A gastric emptying study confirms severe diabetic gastroparesis. Her current medications include metformin 1000 mg twice daily and insulin glargine. Her HbA1c is 8.2%. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in her pharmacological management?

options.A: Initiate a GLP-1 receptor agonist (e.g., semaglutide) to improve glycemic control and promote weight loss, as her HbA1c is elevated and she has excess weight to lose.
options.B: Start a DPP-4 inhibitor (e.g., sitagliptin) as a glucose-lowering agent that is specifically indicated and preferred for patients with gastroparesis.
options.C: Prescribe metoclopramide only as needed for severe vomiting episodes, as continuous use is unnecessary and carries no significant risk of tardive dyskinesia in diabetic patients.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several large, well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on both shins, consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum (NLD). She is concerned about the appearance and occasional ulceration. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial management approach?

options.A: Initiate high-potency topical corticosteroids with occlusion and advise her that this will completely resolve the lesions if she achieves tighter glycemic control with an A1C below 6.5%.
options.B: Perform an immediate incisional biopsy of the largest plaque to confirm the diagnosis, as NLD is a known precursor to squamous cell carcinoma requiring histological surveillance.
options.C: Prescribe a course of systemic immunosuppressants like methotrexate as first-line therapy, given the extensive and cosmetically distressing nature of the lesions, to prevent inevitable progression.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on both shins, diagnosed as necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum (NLD). She is concerned about the appearance and asks about management. Which of the following represents an appropriate initial management approach?

options.A: Initiate high-potency topical corticosteroids with occlusion and advise that this will completely resolve the lesions and prevent ulceration.
options.B: Recommend immediate surgical excision of all lesions to prevent malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma, which is common in NLD.
options.C: Prescribe a course of oral isotretinoin as first-line therapy, as NLD is primarily a disorder of abnormal lipid deposition in the dermis.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents to the podiatry clinic with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot. On examination, there is a deep ulcer that probes to bone, with surrounding erythema and purulent drainage. There is no exposed bone visible, and no gangrene is present. According to the Wagner classification system for diabetic foot ulcers, what is the correct grade for this lesion?

options.A: This is Wagner Grade 2, as the ulcer is deep and probes to bone, which is the defining feature of this grade.
options.B: This is Wagner Grade 3, because the presence of purulent drainage and deep infection indicates osteomyelitis, which defines this grade.
options.C: This is Wagner Grade 1, as the ulcer is deep but there is no exposed bone, gangrene, or abscess formation yet.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents to the podiatry clinic. She has a painless, non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot for 3 months. On examination, the ulcer measures 2.5 cm in diameter, extends to the subcutaneous tissue, and has a small amount of serous drainage. There is no exposed bone, tendon, or joint capsule. There is no surrounding cellulitis or signs of systemic infection. According to the Wagner classification system for diabetic foot ulcers, what is the correct grade for this ulcer?

options.A: Grade 1, because the ulcer is superficial and involves only the epidermis and dermis.
options.B: Grade 2, as the ulcer extends to the bone, which is typical for ulcers of this duration in diabetic patients.
options.C: Grade 3, due to the presence of deep tissue infection and osteomyelitis, which should be presumed in all non-healing diabetic foot ulcers.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents with a fever and body aches. She asks about taking acetaminophen for symptom relief and its potential effect on her CGM readings. Which of the following statements about acetaminophen interference with CGM technology is correct?

options.A: Acetaminophen causes falsely elevated glucose readings on all CGM systems, so she should double-check with fingerstick readings and consider temporarily increasing her insulin doses.
options.B: Only older CGM models are affected by acetaminophen interference; all current-generation CGMs are completely immune to this effect, so she can take it without any concern.
options.C: Acetaminophen causes falsely low glucose readings on all CGM systems, so she should disregard any hypoglycemia alarms and rely solely on symptoms to guide treatment.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a warm, erythematous, and swollen left foot. He reports no history of trauma and minimal pain despite the significant swelling. His HbA1c is 8.2%. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach?

options.A: Immediately start broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics for presumed cellulitis, as the warmth and redness indicate an active infection requiring urgent treatment.
options.B: Prescribe a course of oral NSAIDs and advise rest, ice, compression, and elevation (RICE protocol) for a presumed acute gout flare or soft tissue injury.
options.C: Perform an urgent incision and drainage at the bedside to relieve pressure, as the swelling suggests a deep abscess that must be decompressed to prevent tissue necrosis.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with severe, symptomatic gastroparesis confirmed by gastric emptying study. Her current diabetes regimen includes insulin pump therapy. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in managing her diabetes pharmacotherapy?

options.A: Initiate a GLP-1 receptor agonist to improve glycemic control and promote weight loss, as these agents are known to slow gastric emptying.
options.B: Switch from insulin pump therapy to multiple daily injections of regular insulin before each meal, as this insulin type is better absorbed with delayed gastric emptying.
options.C: Add pramlintide to her insulin regimen to reduce postprandial glucose excursions, as it works synergistically with insulin in gastroparesis.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks gestation is diagnosed with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) and will be using continuous glucose monitoring (CGM). When discussing glycemic targets with her, which of the following time-in-range (TIR) goals should be recommended?

options.A: The primary goal should be to maintain glucose levels below 140 mg/dL for at least 90% of the time, as this matches the threshold for the oral glucose tolerance test.
options.B: She should aim for a time-in-range of 70-180 mg/dL for >70% of readings, which is the same standard target used for non-pregnant adults with type 2 diabetes.
options.C: Given the increased risk of hypoglycemia in pregnancy, the target range should be widened to 80-200 mg/dL to ensure safety, with a goal of >60% TIR.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old patient at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) for self-management. During a telehealth visit, she asks what her primary time in range (TIR) target should be. Based on current international consensus guidelines for GDM, which of the following is the recommended CGM-based TIR target?

options.A: The target should be the same as for non-pregnant adults with type 2 diabetes: >70% of readings between 70-180 mg/dL (3.9-10.0 mmol/L).
options.B: Given the risks of fetal macrosomia, the target should be very strict: >90% of readings between 70-110 mg/dL (3.9-6.1 mmol/L).
options.C: Since she is diet-controlled, targets can be more relaxed; she should aim for >50% of readings between 70-140 mg/dL (3.9-7.8 mmol/L).
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents to urgent care with fever and body aches. She reports taking acetaminophen 1000mg every 6 hours for the past 24 hours. Her CGM readings show persistent values of 180-220 mg/dL, but her fingerstick glucometer readings are 90-110 mg/dL. When counseling her about this discrepancy, which of the following would be appropriate advice?

options.A: Continue using the CGM readings as they are more accurate during illness, and adjust insulin doses based on these elevated values to prevent hyperglycemia.
options.B: Discontinue the CGM sensor immediately and rely solely on fingerstick testing, as acetaminophen permanently damages the sensor's electrochemical components.
options.C: Switch to taking ibuprofen instead of acetaminophen, as all NSAIDs are completely safe with CGM use and won't cause any glucose reading interference.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a warm, swollen, erythematous left foot. He reports no recent trauma and minimal pain. He is afebrile, and his inflammatory markers are only mildly elevated. A provisional diagnosis of acute Charcot neuroarthropathy is considered. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?

options.A: Immediately start a 6-week course of high-dose oral antibiotics for suspected osteomyelitis, as the warm, swollen foot with erythema is pathognomonic for infection.
options.B: Perform urgent surgical debridement and drainage of the foot to relieve pressure and prevent further joint destruction, as this is the standard first-line intervention for acute Charcot foot.
options.C: Apply a compression bandage and advise the patient to continue weight-bearing to maintain muscle tone and circulation, while monitoring for changes over the next 2-3 weeks.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing glucose values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick readings for the past 24 hours. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) for immune support two days ago. What is the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to discontinue her CGM immediately as vitamin C permanently damages the sensor's electrochemical components, making it unsafe for continued use.
options.B: Tell her this is expected and harmless; she should simply subtract 50 mg/dL from all CGM readings while taking vitamin C supplements to obtain accurate values.
options.C: Recommend switching to fingerstick monitoring exclusively while taking vitamin C, as CGM technology cannot provide any useful data when vitamin C interference is present.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old patient at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her fasting glucose is 105 mg/dL and 1-hour postprandial values are consistently 150-165 mg/dL. She is concerned about medication safety. Which of the following statements regarding pharmacologic management is correct?

options.A: Metformin is contraindicated in all trimesters of pregnancy due to proven teratogenic effects and should be avoided in favor of starting basal insulin immediately.
options.B: Metformin is safe throughout pregnancy but should only be used if the patient's HbA1c is above 8.0%, as lower values indicate adequate dietary control without need for medication.
options.C: While metformin crosses the placenta, it is the preferred first-line agent over insulin because it prevents fetal macrosomia more effectively and has no associated neonatal risks.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her fasting glucose is 110 mg/dL and 1-hour postprandial values are consistently 150-160 mg/dL. She asks about medication options. Which of the following statements regarding metformin use in pregnancy is correct?

options.A: Metformin is absolutely contraindicated throughout pregnancy due to proven teratogenic effects in the first trimester.
options.B: Metformin should only be initiated in the third trimester after fetal organogenesis is complete, as it crosses the placenta and may cause fetal hypoglycemia earlier in pregnancy.
options.C: Metformin is the preferred first-line pharmacological agent for GDM because it does not cross the placenta and has superior glycemic control compared to insulin.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a real-time continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports persistently high sensor readings despite feeling well and having normal fingerstick glucose checks. She mentions she recently started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) for immune support. Which of the following statements about this situation is correct?

options.A: Vitamin C causes falsely low CGM readings because it competes with glucose for oxidation at the sensor electrode, so her actual glucose is likely higher than the CGM shows.
options.B: The vitamin C interference is only relevant for older CGM models; all current-generation sensors are completely unaffected by vitamin C supplementation at any dose.
options.C: She should immediately stop her CGM use and switch to fingerstick monitoring exclusively until she completes her course of vitamin C supplements.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents with worsening postprandial nausea, early satiety, and intermittent vomiting over 6 months. She has documented gastroparesis confirmed by gastric emptying scintigraphy. Her current medications include metformin and a basal insulin. Her HbA1c is 8.5%. Which of the following would be an appropriate next step in her diabetes management?

options.A: Add a GLP-1 receptor agonist to improve glycemic control and promote weight loss, as these agents are first-line for patients with gastroparesis.
options.B: Start a DPP-4 inhibitor, as they are specifically indicated for diabetic gastroparesis and will accelerate gastric emptying.
options.C: Switch metformin to an SGLT2 inhibitor, as these agents are recommended for gastroparesis due to their neutral effect on gastrointestinal motility.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on her anterior shins, consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum. She is concerned about the appearance and occasional ulceration. Which of the following represents appropriate management advice?

options.A: Recommend immediate high-potency topical corticosteroids with occlusion to all lesions, as this is the definitive first-line treatment that reliably induces remission.
options.B: Advise that strict glycemic control with an A1C target of <6.0% is the primary treatment, as this will directly reverse the skin changes and prevent progression.
options.C: Prescribe a course of oral tetracycline antibiotics, as the lesions are primarily caused by subclinical bacterial infection requiring systemic antimicrobial therapy.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several large, well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on both shins, consistent with necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum. She is concerned about the appearance and occasional ulceration. What is the most appropriate initial management approach?

options.A: Initiate high-dose oral corticosteroids immediately to suppress the inflammatory process and prevent further skin breakdown.
options.B: Recommend complete bed rest with leg elevation for 4-6 weeks, as weight-bearing is the primary cause of progression in these lesions.
options.C: Perform an urgent skin biopsy of all lesions to confirm the diagnosis, as visual diagnosis is unreliable and malignancy must be ruled out first.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with type 2 diabetes for 15 years presents to the clinic with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of his right foot. On examination, there is a deep ulcer that exposes the underlying tendon, but there is no bone involvement, abscess, or osteomyelitis. According to the Wagner classification system for diabetic foot ulcers, what is the correct grade for this lesion?

options.A: This is Wagner grade 2, as any ulcer that exposes tendon or joint capsule is automatically classified as grade 2, regardless of depth or infection.
options.B: This is Wagner grade 3, because once an ulcer extends to tendon or joint capsule, it indicates deep tissue involvement equivalent to grade 3.
options.C: This is Wagner grade 1, since the ulcer is still confined to the skin and subcutaneous tissue, and tendon exposure without bone involvement does not change the grade.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents to the podiatry clinic with a non-healing ulcer on the plantar surface of her right foot. On examination, you note a deep ulcer that extends down to bone, with surrounding erythema and warmth. There is no purulent drainage or crepitus. According to the Wagner classification system for diabetic foot ulcers, what is the grade of this ulcer?

options.A: Grade 2, because the ulcer extends to bone and there is no gangrene present.
options.B: Grade 3, as the presence of bone involvement automatically indicates deep tissue abscess or osteomyelitis.
options.C: Grade 1, since the ulcer is deep but there are no systemic signs of infection like fever or leukocytosis.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a Dexcom G6 CGM reports to her endocrinologist that she has been taking acetaminophen 1000mg every 6 hours for a recent back strain. She is concerned about potential interference with her CGM readings. Which of the following statements about this interaction is correct?

options.A: Acetaminophen causes a predictable downward drift in CGM readings, so she should add 20 mg/dL to all her sensor glucose values while taking this medication.
options.B: The Dexcom G6 uses a different enzyme technology that is completely immune to acetaminophen interference, so she can continue taking it without any concerns about her CGM accuracy.
options.C: Acetaminophen interference is only clinically significant at doses above 4000mg per day, so her current 4000mg daily dose is exactly at the threshold where no adjustment is needed.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a warm, erythematous, and swollen left foot. He reports no history of trauma and minimal pain. His HbA1c is 8.2%. Based on the clinical suspicion of acute Charcot neuroarthropathy, which of the following is the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach?

options.A: Immediately start intravenous antibiotics for presumed cellulitis and obtain an X-ray only if symptoms persist after 48 hours of treatment.
options.B: Perform a bedside incision and drainage to rule out an abscess, as the warmth and swelling are highly suggestive of a deep-space infection requiring surgical intervention.
options.C: Advise the patient to continue weight-bearing and obtain a follow-up X-ray in 4-6 weeks, as early Charcot changes are often not visible on initial imaging.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and severe gastroparesis (confirmed by gastric emptying study) presents for diabetes management review. Her current HbA1c is 8.5% despite metformin and basal insulin. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in her pharmacologic management?

options.A: Initiate a GLP-1 receptor agonist to improve glycemic control and promote weight loss, as gastroparesis is not a contraindication.
options.B: Switch from metformin to a sulfonylurea to avoid gastrointestinal side effects, as metformin likely exacerbates her gastroparesis symptoms.
options.C: Add pramlintide to her regimen to slow gastric emptying and improve postprandial glucose, as this is specifically indicated for diabetic gastroparesis.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is reviewing her continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) data. Her obstetrician asks you, the diabetes educator, what the recommended time-in-range (TIR) target is for her pregnancy. Which of the following represents the correct TIR target for GDM?

options.A: The target should be identical to non-pregnant adults with type 2 diabetes: >70% of readings between 70-180 mg/dL (3.9-10.0 mmol/L).
options.B: Given the lower hypoglycemia risk in GDM, the target should be >85% of readings between 90-200 mg/dL (5.0-11.1 mmol/L) to minimize fetal hyperglycemia exposure.
options.C: For diet-controlled GDM, strict targets aren't necessary; maintaining >50% of readings below 140 mg/dL (7.8 mmol/L) at 1-hour postprandial is sufficient.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old woman at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM). During her clinic visit, you review her ambulatory glucose profile. According to current international consensus guidelines for GDM, what is the recommended primary CGM target for time in range (TIR) during pregnancy?

options.A: The primary target should be >90% time in the range of 3.9-7.8 mmol/L (70-140 mg/dL), with less than 1% time below range.
options.B: The primary target should be >80% time in the range of 4.0-6.7 mmol/L (72-120 mg/dL), as this stricter range prevents macrosomia without increasing hypoglycemia risk.
options.C: The primary target should be >70% time in the range of 3.5-8.5 mmol/L (63-153 mg/dL), matching the general adult diabetes target, as pregnancy does not require stricter control.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes uses a Dexcom G6 continuous glucose monitor (CGM). She develops a headache and takes 1000 mg of acetaminophen. Which of the following statements about the potential effect on her CGM readings is correct?

options.A: Acetaminophen will cause the CGM to read falsely low, so she should add 20 mg/dL to all readings for the next 6 hours.
options.B: The Dexcom G6 is completely immune to acetaminophen interference, so no special precautions are needed regardless of dose.
options.C: She should temporarily switch to fingerstick glucose monitoring for 24 hours after taking acetaminophen, as the CGM readings will be completely unreliable during this period.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 52-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a warm, swollen, erythematous left foot. He reports no recent trauma and minimal pain. His temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), and he has significant peripheral neuropathy. Based on the suspicion of acute Charcot neuroarthropathy, which of the following represents the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach?

options.A: Immediate surgical debridement and biopsy of the swollen area to rule out osteomyelitis, as this is the fastest way to confirm the diagnosis and prevent bone destruction.
options.B: Start a 10-day course of broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics for presumed cellulitis or osteomyelitis, as the warmth and redness are classic signs of infection that must be treated first.
options.C: Apply a compression bandage to reduce the swelling, advise weight-bearing as tolerated to maintain mobility, and schedule a follow-up X-ray in 4-6 weeks to see if any bony changes develop.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports that her sensor readings have been consistently showing glucose values 40-60 mg/dL higher than her fingerstick readings for the past 2 days. She mentions she started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (1000 mg twice daily) for immune support 3 days ago. Which of the following represents the most appropriate clinical advice regarding this discrepancy?

options.A: Advise her to stop the vitamin C supplements immediately, as they are known to cause falsely low CGM readings by interfering with the sensor's electrochemical signal.
options.B: Tell her to continue both monitoring methods but add a correction factor of 50 mg/dL to all CGM readings while she's taking vitamin C, as this interference is predictable and consistent across all CGM brands.
options.C: Recommend switching to a different brand of CGM that uses optical sensing technology, as these are immune to vitamin C interference unlike electrochemical sensors.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 32-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her BMI is 28 kg/m². She is concerned about medication safety for her baby. Which of the following statements about initiating metformin in this patient is correct?

options.A: Metformin is absolutely contraindicated in all trimesters of pregnancy due to a high risk of major congenital malformations, and insulin should be the only pharmacological option considered.
options.B: Metformin is safe to initiate, but it should be stopped immediately upon delivery as it can cause severe hypoglycemia in the newborn if present in breast milk.
options.C: Metformin is preferred over insulin as first-line pharmacotherapy because it crosses the placenta and directly helps regulate fetal blood glucose, reducing the risk of macrosomia.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation with newly diagnosed gestational diabetes (GDM) has persistent hyperglycemia despite dietary modification. Her obstetrician is considering adding metformin to her management plan. Which of the following statements about metformin use in pregnancy is accurate?

options.A: Metformin should be avoided entirely in pregnancy as it is FDA Category X and known to cause major congenital malformations when used during the first trimester.
options.B: Metformin is safe throughout pregnancy but must be discontinued at 36 weeks gestation to prevent neonatal lactic acidosis during delivery.
options.C: Metformin is the preferred first-line pharmacological agent over insulin for all patients with GDM because it does not cross the placenta and has no fetal effects.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a real-time continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reports persistently high sensor readings despite feeling well and having normal fingerstick glucose checks. She mentions she recently started taking high-dose vitamin C supplements (2,000 mg daily) for immune support. Regarding the CGM accuracy issue, which of the following is the most appropriate clinical advice?

options.A: Advise her to continue the vitamin C supplements but double-check all CGM readings with fingerstick glucose for the next 48 hours, after which the interference should resolve as her body adjusts.
options.B: Tell her to stop the vitamin C immediately and calibrate her CGM sensor with a fingerstick reading; the falsely elevated readings are caused by vitamin C oxidizing the sensor's glucose oxidase enzyme.
options.C: Explain that vitamin C causes falsely low CGM readings by competing with glucose at the sensor electrode, so her actual glucose levels are likely higher than the sensor shows, and she should increase her insulin doses accordingly.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 34-year-old patient at 28 weeks gestation with diet-controlled gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) for self-management. When reviewing her ambulatory glucose profile, which of the following represents the correct consensus target for time in range (TIR) during pregnancy?

options.A: The primary goal should be >70% of readings between 70-140 mg/dL, as this is the standard non-pregnancy target and is safe for fetal development.
options.B: Aim for >80% of readings between 90-120 mg/dL, as tighter control in this narrow range minimizes the risk of fetal macrosomia.
options.C: Target >50% of readings below 95 mg/dL fasting and 120 mg/dL postprandial, as this matches the older capillary blood glucose thresholds used in pregnancy.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes for 25 years presents with several well-demarcated, yellowish-brown plaques with central atrophy and telangiectasias on both shins, diagnosed as necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum. She is concerned about the appearance and asks about management. Which of the following represents appropriate initial management?

options.A: Start high-potency topical corticosteroids with occlusion and advise her that this will completely resolve the lesions within 2-3 weeks.
options.B: Recommend immediate surgical excision of all lesions to prevent malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma, which is a common complication.
options.C: Initiate systemic immunosuppression with oral prednisone 40mg daily as first-line therapy, as this condition indicates severe, uncontrolled systemic inflammation from her diabetes.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) presents with a fever and body aches. She asks if she can safely take acetaminophen for symptom relief while wearing her CGM. Which of the following represents appropriate guidance?

options.A: Acetaminophen causes falsely low CGM readings, so she should double-check any low glucose values with a fingerstick before treating hypoglycemia.
options.B: All modern CGMs are completely immune to acetaminophen interference, so she can take any dose without concern for CGM accuracy.
options.C: She should take ibuprofen instead, as NSAIDs do not interfere with CGM readings, unlike acetaminophen which causes permanent sensor damage.
options.D: None of the above

question: A 42-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes using a Dexcom G6 CGM reports that her sensor readings have been showing values around 250 mg/dL for several hours, despite feeling well. She mentions she took 1000 mg of acetaminophen 4 hours ago for a tension headache. Her fingerstick glucose check shows 120 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate clinical interpretation and action regarding this discrepancy?
